**Jordan**,[[a]](#cite_note-8) officially the **Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan**,[[b]](#cite_note-9) is a country in the [Southern Levant](/wiki/Southern_Levant "Southern Levant") region of [West Asia](/wiki/West_Asia "West Asia"). Jordan is bordered by [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria") to the north, [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq") to the east, [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") to the south, and both [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") and [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine "Palestine") ([West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank")) to the west. The [Jordan River](/wiki/Jordan_River "Jordan River"), flowing into the [Dead Sea](/wiki/Dead_Sea "Dead Sea"), is located along the country's western border within the [Jordan Rift Valley](/wiki/Jordan_Rift_Valley "Jordan Rift Valley"). Jordan has a small coastline along the [Red Sea](/wiki/Red_Sea "Red Sea") in its southwest, separated by the [Gulf of Aqaba](/wiki/Gulf_of_Aqaba "Gulf of Aqaba") from [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"). [Amman](/wiki/Amman "Amman") is the country's capital and [largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Jordan "List of cities in Jordan"), as well as the [most populous city in the Levant](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities_in_the_Levant_region_by_population "List of largest cities in the Levant region by population").

Inhabited by humans since the [Paleolithic](/wiki/Paleolithic "Paleolithic") period, three kingdoms developed in [Transjordan](/wiki/Transjordan_(region) "Transjordan (region)") during the [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age"): [Ammon](/wiki/Ammon "Ammon"), [Moab](/wiki/Moab "Moab") and [Edom](/wiki/Edom "Edom"). In the third century BC, the Arab [Nabataeans](/wiki/Nabataeans "Nabataeans") established [their kingdom](/wiki/Nabataean_Kingdom "Nabataean Kingdom") centered in [Petra](/wiki/Petra "Petra"). The [Greco-Roman](/wiki/Greco-Roman_world "Greco-Roman world") period saw the establishment of several cities in Transjordan that comprised the [Decapolis](/wiki/Decapolis "Decapolis"). After the end of [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") rule, the region became part of the Islamic caliphates of the [Rashidun](/wiki/Rashidun_Caliphate "Rashidun Caliphate"), [Umayyad](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate"), [Abbasid](/wiki/Abbasid_Caliphate "Abbasid Caliphate"), and the [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"). Following the 1916 [Great Arab Revolt](/wiki/Great_Arab_Revolt "Great Arab Revolt") during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), former [Ottoman Syria](/wiki/Syria_vilayet "Syria vilayet") was [partitioned](/wiki/Partition_of_the_Ottoman_Empire#British_Mandates "Partition of the Ottoman Empire"), leading to the [establishment](/wiki/Establishment_of_the_Emirate_of_Transjordan "Establishment of the Emirate of Transjordan") of the [Emirate of Transjordan](/wiki/Emirate_of_Transjordan "Emirate of Transjordan") in 1921, which became a British protectorate. In 1946, Jordan gained independence and became officially known as the Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan.[[c]](#cite_note-10) Jordan [captured and annexed](/wiki/Jordanian_annexation_of_the_West_Bank "Jordanian annexation of the West Bank") the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank") during the [1948 Palestine war](/wiki/1948_Palestine_war "1948 Palestine war") until it was occupied by Israel [in 1967](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War"). Jordan [renounced](/wiki/Jordanian_disengagement_from_the_West_Bank "Jordanian disengagement from the West Bank") its claim to the territory to the [Palestinians](/wiki/Palestine_Liberation_Organization "Palestine Liberation Organization") in 1988 and signed a [peace treaty with Israel](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93Jordan_peace_treaty "Israel–Jordan peace treaty") in 1994.

Jordan is a [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") country, covering an area of 89,342 km2 (34,495 sq mi) with a population of 11.5 million, making it the [eleventh-most populous Arab country](/wiki/List_of_Arab_League_countries_by_population "List of Arab League countries by population"). The dominant majority, or around 95% of the country's population, is [Sunni Muslim](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam"), with the rest being mostly [Arab Christian](/wiki/Christianity_in_Jordan "Christianity in Jordan"). Jordan was mostly unscathed by the violence that swept the region following the [Arab Spring](/wiki/Arab_Spring "Arab Spring") in 2010. From as early as 1948, Jordan has accepted refugees from multiple neighbouring countries in conflict. An estimated 2.1 million [Palestinian refugees](/wiki/Palestinian_refugees "Palestinian refugees"), most of whom hold Jordanian citizenship, as well as 1.4 million [Syrian refugees](/wiki/Syrian_refugees "Syrian refugees"), were residing in Jordan in 2015.[[4]](#cite_note-census-4) The kingdom is also a refuge for thousands of [Christian Iraqis](/wiki/Christianity_in_Iraq "Christianity in Iraq") fleeing persecution.[[8]](#cite_note-11)[[9]](#cite_note-12) While Jordan continues to accept refugees, the large Syrian influx during the 2010s has placed substantial strain on national resources and infrastructure.[[10]](#cite_note-UNHCR-13)

The sovereign state is a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"), but the king holds wide executive and legislative powers. Jordan is a founding member of the [Arab League](/wiki/Arab_League "Arab League") and the [Organisation of Islamic Cooperation](/wiki/Organisation_of_Islamic_Cooperation "Organisation of Islamic Cooperation"). Jordan has a high [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index"), ranking 100th, and is considered a lower middle income economy. The [Jordanian economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Jordan "Economy of Jordan"), one of the smallest economies in the region, is attractive to foreign investors based upon a skilled workforce.[[11]](#cite_note-skwf-14) Jordan is a major tourist destination, also attracting medical tourism with [its well-developed health sector](/wiki/Health_in_Jordan "Health in Jordan").[[12]](#cite_note-tourists-15) Nonetheless, a lack of natural resources, large flow of refugees, and regional turmoil have hampered economic growth.[[13]](#cite_note-washington-16)

## Etymology

See also: [Jordan River § Etymology](/wiki/Jordan_River#Etymology "Jordan River")

Jordan takes its name from the [Jordan River](/wiki/Jordan_River "Jordan River"), which forms much of Jordan's northwestern border.[[14]](#cite_note-CIA_factbook-17) While several theories for the origin of the river's name have been proposed (such as relation to the [Egyptian](/wiki/Egyptian_language "Egyptian language") loanword 'yǝʾor'; 'big river', the [Nile](/wiki/Nile "Nile")),[[15]](#cite_note-rahkonen-18) it is most plausible that it derives from the [Semitic](/wiki/Semitic_languages "Semitic languages") word 'Yarad' ( ירד ; 'flow down' or 'descend"), referring to the river's declivity[[16]](#cite_note-etm1-19) and with cognates found in [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"), [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic_language "Aramaic language"), [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"), [Mandaic](/wiki/Mandaic_language "Mandaic language"), etc.[[16]](#cite_note-etm1-19)

The earliest use of the name is found in the ancient Egyptian [Papyrus Anastasi I](/wiki/Papyrus_Anastasi_I "Papyrus Anastasi I") as *Yārdon*, probably dating to the reign of [Rameses II](/wiki/Rameses_II "Rameses II").[[17]](#cite_note-20) Much of the area that makes up modern Jordan was historically called *[Transjordan](/wiki/Transjordan_(region) "Transjordan (region)")*, meaning "across the Jordan"; the term is used to denote the lands east of the river.[[16]](#cite_note-etm1-19) The [Hebrew Bible](/wiki/Hebrew_Bible "Hebrew Bible") uses the term עבר הירדן *Ever ha'Yarden* (lit. 'the other side of the Jordan') for the area.[[16]](#cite_note-etm1-19) Early Arab chronicles call the river *Al-Urdunn* (a term cognate to the Hebrew *Yarden*).[[18]](#cite_note-etm2-21) *[Jund Al-Urdunn](/wiki/Jund_Al-Urdunn "Jund Al-Urdunn")* was a military district around the river in the early Islamic era.[[18]](#cite_note-etm2-21) Later, during the [Crusades](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Jerusalem "Kingdom of Jerusalem") in the beginning of the second millennium, a lordship was established in the area under the name of *[Oultrejordain](/wiki/Oultrejordain "Oultrejordain")*.[[19]](#cite_note-22)

## History

Main article: [History of Jordan](/wiki/History_of_Jordan "History of Jordan")

### Ancient period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/20100923_amman37.JPG/250px-20100923_amman37.JPG)](/wiki/File:20100923_amman37.JPG)

The ['Ain Ghazal Statues](/wiki/%27Ain_Ghazal_Statues "'Ain Ghazal Statues"), c. 7250 BC, uncovered in Amman, are some of the oldest human statues ever found.

The oldest known evidence of [hominid](/wiki/Hominid "Hominid") habitation in Jordan dates back at least 200,000 years.[[20]](#cite_note-KOJ-23) Jordan is a rich source of [Paleolithic](/wiki/Paleolithic "Paleolithic") human remains (up to 20,000 years old) due to its location within the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant"), where [migrations out of Africa](/wiki/Recent_African_origin_of_modern_humans "Recent African origin of modern humans") converged,[[21]](#cite_note-chap-paleolithic-24) and its more humid climate during the [Late Pleistocene](/wiki/Late_Pleistocene "Late Pleistocene"), which resulted in the formation of numerous remains-preserving wetlands in the region.[[22]](#cite_note-Abu-JaberEtAl2020PPP-25) Past lakeshore environments attracted different groups of hominids, and several remains of tools dating from the Late Pleistocene have been found there.[[21]](#cite_note-chap-paleolithic-24) Scientists have found the world's oldest known evidence of bread-making at a 14,500-year-old [Natufian](/wiki/Natufian_culture "Natufian culture") site in Jordan's northeastern desert.[[23]](#cite_note-26)

During the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") period (10,000–4,500 BC), there was a transition there from a [hunter-gatherer](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer") culture to a culture with established populous agricultural villages.[[24]](#cite_note-27) ['Ain Ghazal](/wiki/Ayn_Ghazal_(archaeological_site) "Ayn Ghazal (archaeological site)"), one such village located at a site in the eastern part of present-day [Amman](/wiki/Amman "Amman"), is one of the largest known prehistoric settlements in the [Near East](/wiki/Near_East "Near East").[[25]](#cite_note-28) [Dozens of plaster statues](/wiki/%27Ain_Ghazal_Statues "'Ain Ghazal Statues") of the human form, dating to 7250 BC or earlier, have been uncovered there. They are one of the oldest large-scale representations of humans ever found.[[26]](#cite_note-aingazal-29) During the [Chalcolithic period](/wiki/Chalcolithic "Chalcolithic") (4500–3600 BC), several villages emerged in Transjordan including [Tulaylet Ghassul](/wiki/Teleilat_el_Ghassul "Teleilat el Ghassul") in the [Jordan Valley](/wiki/Jordan_Valley "Jordan Valley");[[27]](#cite_note-30) a series of circular stone enclosures in the eastern basalt desert from the same period have long baffled archaeologists.[[28]](#cite_note-31)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Mesha_Stele_%28511142469%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Mesha_Stele_%28511142469%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mesha_Stele_(511142469)_(cropped).jpg)

The [Mesha Stele](/wiki/Mesha_Stele "Mesha Stele") (c. 840 BC) records the glory of [Mesha](/wiki/Mesha "Mesha"), King of [Moab](/wiki/Moab "Moab"), displayed at the [Louvre Museum](/wiki/Louvre_Museum "Louvre Museum").

Fortified towns and urban centres first emerged in the southern Levant early in the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") (3600–1200 BC).[[29]](#cite_note-atlas11-32) [Wadi Feynan](/wiki/Wadi_Feynan "Wadi Feynan") became a regional centre for copper extraction: the metal was exploited on a large scale to produce bronze.[[30]](#cite_note-33) Trade and movement of people in the Middle East peaked, causing cultural innovations and whole civilisations to spread.[[31]](#cite_note-atlas12-34) Villages in Transjordan expanded rapidly in areas with reliable water-resources and arable land.[[31]](#cite_note-atlas12-34) [Ancient Egyptian](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") populations expanded towards the Levant and came to control both banks of the Jordan River.[[32]](#cite_note-atlas13-35)

During the [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age") (1200–332 BC), after the withdrawal of the Egyptians, Transjordan was home to the kingdoms of [Ammon](/wiki/Ammon "Ammon"), [Edom](/wiki/Edom "Edom") and [Moab](/wiki/Moab "Moab").[[33]](#cite_note-AEM-36) These peoples spoke [Semitic languages](/wiki/Semitic_languages "Semitic languages") of the [Canaanite group](/wiki/Canaanite_languages "Canaanite languages"). Archaeologists have concluded that their polities were tribal kingdoms rather than states.[[33]](#cite_note-AEM-36) Ammon was located in the Amman plateau; Moab in the highlands east of the Dead Sea; and Edom in the area around [Wadi Araba](/wiki/Arabah "Arabah") in the south.[[33]](#cite_note-AEM-36) The northwestern region of the Transjordan, known then as [Gilead](/wiki/Gilead "Gilead"), was settled by the [Israelites](/wiki/Israelites "Israelites").[[34]](#cite_note-37)

The three kingdoms continually clashed with the neighbouring Hebrew kingdoms of [Israel](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Israel_(Samaria) "Kingdom of Israel (Samaria)") and [Judah](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Judah "Kingdom of Judah"), centred west of the Jordan River.[[35]](#cite_note-harrison-38) One record of this is the [Mesha Stele](/wiki/Mesha_Stele "Mesha Stele"), erected by the Moabite king [Mesha](/wiki/Mesha "Mesha") in 840 BC. In an inscription on it, he lauds himself for the building projects that he initiated in Moab and commemorates his glory and his victory against the Israelites.[[36]](#cite_note-rollston-39) The stele constitutes one of the most important archeological parallels to [accounts recorded in the Bible](/wiki/Historicity_of_the_Bible "Historicity of the Bible").[[37]](#cite_note-mesha-louvre-40) At the same time, Israel and the Kingdom of [Aram-Damascus](/wiki/Aram-Damascus "Aram-Damascus") competed for control of the Gilead.[[38]](#cite_note-41)[[39]](#cite_note-42)

Around 740–720 BC, Israel and Aram-Damascus were conquered by the [Neo-Assyrian Empire](/wiki/Neo-Assyrian_Empire "Neo-Assyrian Empire"). The kingdoms of Ammon, Edom and Moab were subjugated but were allowed to maintain some degree of independence.[[40]](#cite_note-chap-iron-43) Then in 627 BC, following after the disintegration of the Assyrians' empire, [Babylonians](/wiki/Neo-Babylonian_Empire "Neo-Babylonian Empire") took control of the area.[[40]](#cite_note-chap-iron-43) Although the kingdoms supported the Babylonians against Judah in the 597 BC [sack of Jerusalem](/wiki/Siege_of_Jerusalem_(597_BC) "Siege of Jerusalem (597 BC)"), they rebelled against Babylon a decade later.[[40]](#cite_note-chap-iron-43) The kingdoms were reduced to [vassals](/wiki/Vassal "Vassal"), a status they retained under the [Persian](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire") and [Hellenic](/wiki/Macedonia_(ancient_kingdom) "Macedonia (ancient kingdom)") empires.[[40]](#cite_note-chap-iron-43) By the beginning of [Roman rule](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") around 63 BC, the kingdoms of Ammon, Edom and Moab had lost their distinct identities and were assimilated into the Roman culture.[[33]](#cite_note-AEM-36) Some Edomites survived longer – driven by the Nabataeans, they had migrated to southern [Judea](/wiki/Judea "Judea"), which became known as [Idumaea](/wiki/Idumaea "Idumaea"). They were later converted to [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") by the [Hasmoneans](/wiki/Hasmonean_dynasty "Hasmonean dynasty").[[41]](#cite_note-44)

### Classical period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Al_Khazneh_Petra_edit_2_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Al_Khazneh_Petra_edit_2_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Al_Khazneh_Petra_edit_2_(cropped).jpg)

[Petra](/wiki/Petra "Petra"), one of the [New 7 Wonders of the World](/wiki/New_7_Wonders_of_the_World "New 7 Wonders of the World"), contains [Al-Khazneh](/wiki/Al-Khazneh "Al-Khazneh"), believed to be the mausoleum of the [Nabataean](/wiki/Nabataean "Nabataean") King [Aretas IV](/wiki/Aretas_IV_Philopatris "Aretas IV Philopatris"), 1st century AD.

[Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great")'s [conquest](/wiki/Wars_of_Alexander_the_Great "Wars of Alexander the Great") of the Persian Empire in 332 BC introduced [Hellenistic culture](/wiki/Hellenistic_period "Hellenistic period") to the Middle East.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199810-45) After Alexander's death in 323 BC, the [empire split](/wiki/Partition_of_Babylon "Partition of Babylon") among his generals, and in the end much of Transjordan was disputed between the [Ptolemies](/wiki/Ptolemaic_Kingdom "Ptolemaic Kingdom") based in Egypt and the [Seleucids](/wiki/Seleucid_Empire "Seleucid Empire") based in Syria.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199810-45) The [Nabataeans](/wiki/Nabataeans "Nabataeans"), nomadic Arabs based south of Edom, managed to establish an independent kingdom in 169 BC by exploiting the struggle between the two Greek powers.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199810-45) The [Nabataean Kingdom](/wiki/Nabataean_Kingdom "Nabataean Kingdom") controlled much of the trade routes of the region, and it stretched south along the [Red Sea](/wiki/Red_Sea "Red Sea") coast into the [Hejaz](/wiki/Hejaz "Hejaz") desert, up to as far north as [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus"), which it controlled for a short period (85–71 BC).[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor200151-46) The Nabataeans massed a fortune from their control of the trade routes, often drawing the envy of their neighbours.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor200130-47) Petra, Nabataea's capital, flourished in the 1st century AD, driven by its extensive water irrigation systems and agriculture.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor200170-48) The Nabataeans were talented [stone carvers](/wiki/Stone_carving "Stone carving"), building their most elaborate structure, [Al-Khazneh](/wiki/Al-Khazneh "Al-Khazneh"), in the first century AD.[[46]](#cite_note-petra-49) It is believed to be the [mausoleum](/wiki/Mausoleum "Mausoleum") of the Arab Nabataean King [Aretas IV](/wiki/Aretas_IV_Philopatris "Aretas IV Philopatris").[[46]](#cite_note-petra-49)

Roman legions under [Pompey](/wiki/Pompey "Pompey") conquered much of the Levant in 63 BC, inaugurating a period of Roman rule that lasted four centuries.[[47]](#cite_note-roman-50) In 106 AD, Emperor [Trajan](/wiki/Trajan "Trajan") annexed Nabataea unopposed and rebuilt the [King's Highway](/wiki/King%27s_Highway_(ancient) "King's Highway (ancient)") which became known as the [Via Traiana Nova](/wiki/Via_Traiana_Nova "Via Traiana Nova") road.[[47]](#cite_note-roman-50) The Romans gave the Greek cities of Transjordan—[Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia_(Amman) "Philadelphia (Amman)") (Amman), [Gerasa](/wiki/Jerash "Jerash") (Jerash), [Gedara](/wiki/Umm_Qays "Umm Qays") (Umm Quays), [Pella](/wiki/Pella,_Jordan "Pella, Jordan") (Tabaqat Fahl) and [Arbila](/wiki/Irbid "Irbid") (Irbid)—and other Hellenistic cities in Palestine and southern Syria, a level of autonomy by forming the *[Decapolis](/wiki/Decapolis "Decapolis")*, a ten-city league.[[48]](#cite_note-atlas15-51) Jerash is one of the best preserved Roman cities in the East; it was even visited by Emperor [Hadrian](/wiki/Hadrian "Hadrian") during his journey to Palestine.[[49]](#cite_note-52)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Jerash_01_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Jerash_01_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jerash_01_(cropped).jpg)

The Oval Forum of [Jerash](/wiki/Jerash "Jerash") (c. 1st century AD), then member of the ten-city Greco-Roman league, the *[Decapolis](/wiki/Decapolis "Decapolis")*, seven of which are present in modern-day Jordan

In 324 AD, the Roman Empire split and the Eastern Roman Empire, later known as the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), continued to control or influence the region until 636.[[50]](#cite_note-byzantine-53) Christianity had [become legal within the empire in 313](/wiki/Edict_of_Milan "Edict of Milan") after co-emperors [Constantine](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great "Constantine the Great") and [Licinius](/wiki/Licinius "Licinius") signed an edict of toleration.[[50]](#cite_note-byzantine-53) In 380, the [Edict of Thessalonica](/wiki/Edict_of_Thessalonica "Edict of Thessalonica") made Christianity the official state religion. Transjordan prospered during the Byzantine era, and Christian churches were built throughout the region.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199814-54)

The [Aqaba Church](/wiki/Aqaba_Church "Aqaba Church") in [Ayla](/wiki/Aqaba "Aqaba") was built during this era. It is considered to be the [world's first purpose built Christian church](/wiki/List_of_oldest_church_buildings "List of oldest church buildings").[[52]](#cite_note-55) [Umm ar-Rasas](/wiki/Umm_ar-Rasas "Umm ar-Rasas") in southern Amman contains at least 16 Byzantine churches.[[53]](#cite_note-56) Meanwhile, Petra's importance declined as sea trade routes emerged, and after a [363 earthquake](/wiki/Galilee_earthquake_of_363 "Galilee earthquake of 363") destroyed many structures it declined further, eventually being abandoned.[[46]](#cite_note-petra-49) The [Sasanian Empire](/wiki/Sasanian_Empire "Sasanian Empire") in the east became the Byzantines' rivals, and [frequent confrontations](/wiki/Byzantine%E2%80%93Sasanian_wars "Byzantine–Sasanian wars") sometimes led to the Sasanids controlling some parts of the region, including Transjordan.[[54]](#cite_note-57)

### Islamic era

In 629, during the [Battle of Mu'tah](/wiki/Battle_of_Mu%27tah "Battle of Mu'tah") in what is today [Karak Governorate](/wiki/Karak_Governorate "Karak Governorate"), the Byzantines and their [Arab Christian clients](/wiki/Patronage_in_ancient_Rome "Patronage in ancient Rome"), the [Ghassanids](/wiki/Ghassanids "Ghassanids"), staved off an attack by a Muslim [Rashidun](/wiki/Rashidun "Rashidun") force that marched northwards towards the Levant from the Hejaz.[[55]](#cite_note-Bowersock-58) The Byzantines however were defeated by the Muslims in 636 at the decisive [Battle of the Yarmuk](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Yarmuk "Battle of the Yarmuk") just north of Transjordan.[[55]](#cite_note-Bowersock-58) Transjordan was an essential territory for the conquest of Damascus.[[56]](#cite_note-mus-59) The Rashidun caliphate was followed by that of the [Umayyads](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate") (661–750).[[56]](#cite_note-mus-59)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/The_Madaba_Map%2C_part_of_a_floor_mosaic_in_the_early_Byzantine_church_of_Saint_George_depicting_the_Holy_Land_in_the_6th_century_AD%2C_Madaba%2C_Jordan_%2834477807231%29.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Madaba_Map,_part_of_a_floor_mosaic_in_the_early_Byzantine_church_of_Saint_George_depicting_the_Holy_Land_in_the_6th_century_AD,_Madaba,_Jordan_(34477807231).jpg)

Byzantine [Madaba Map](/wiki/Madaba_Map "Madaba Map") showing the Jordan River and the Dead Sea. Dating to the 6th century AD, it is the oldest surviving depiction of the [Holy Land](/wiki/Holy_Land "Holy Land").

Under the Umayyad Caliphate, several [desert castles](/wiki/Desert_castles "Desert castles") were constructed in Transjordan, including [Qasr Al-Mshatta](/wiki/Qasr_Al-Mshatta "Qasr Al-Mshatta") and [Qasr Al-Hallabat](/wiki/Qasr_Al-Hallabat "Qasr Al-Hallabat").[[56]](#cite_note-mus-59) The [Abbasid Caliphate](/wiki/Abbasid_Caliphate "Abbasid Caliphate")'s campaign to take over the Umayyad's began in a village in Transjordan known as [Humayma](/wiki/Humayma "Humayma").[[57]](#cite_note-atlas-60) The powerful [749 earthquake](/wiki/749_Galilee_earthquake "749 Galilee earthquake") is thought to have contributed to the Umayyads' [defeat by the Abbasids](/wiki/Abbasid_revolution "Abbasid revolution"), who moved the caliphate's capital from Damascus to [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad").[[57]](#cite_note-atlas-60) During Abbasid rule (750–969), several Arab tribes moved northwards and settled in the Levant.[[56]](#cite_note-mus-59) As had happened during the Roman era, growth of maritime trade diminished Transjordan's central position, and the area became increasingly impoverished.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199821-61) After the decline of the Abbasids, Transjordan was ruled by the [Fatimid Caliphate](/wiki/Fatimid_Caliphate "Fatimid Caliphate") (969–1070), then by the [Crusader](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades") [Kingdom of Jerusalem](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Jerusalem "Kingdom of Jerusalem") (1115–1187).[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199822-62)

The Crusaders constructed several castles as part of the Lordship of Oultrejordain, including [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal_(castle) "Montreal (castle)") and Al-Karak.[[60]](#cite_note-63) During the [Battle of Hattin](/wiki/Battle_of_Hattin "Battle of Hattin") (1187) near [Lake Tiberias](/wiki/Sea_of_Galilee "Sea of Galilee") just north of Transjordan, the Crusaders lost to [Saladin](/wiki/Saladin "Saladin"), the founder of the [Ayyubid dynasty](/wiki/Ayyubid_dynasty "Ayyubid dynasty") (1187–1260).[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199823-64) The Ayyubids built the [Ajloun Castle](/wiki/Ajloun_Castle "Ajloun Castle") and rebuilt older castles to be used as military outposts against the Crusaders.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199823-64) Villages in Transjordan under the Ayyubids became important stops for Muslim pilgrims going to [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca "Mecca") who travelled along the route that connected Syria to the Hejaz.[[62]](#cite_note-atlas1-65) Several of the Ayyubid castles were used and expanded by the [Mamluks](/wiki/Mamluk_Sultanate "Mamluk Sultanate") (1260–1516), who divided Transjordan between the provinces of Karak and Damascus.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199825-66) During the next century Transjordan experienced [Mongol](/wiki/Mongol_Empire "Mongol Empire") attacks, but the Mongols were ultimately repelled by the Mamluks at the [Battle of Ain Jalut](/wiki/Battle_of_Ain_Jalut "Battle of Ain Jalut") (1260).[[64]](#cite_note-67)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Kerak_BW_1_%28cropped%29.JPG/250px-Kerak_BW_1_%28cropped%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Kerak_BW_1_(cropped).JPG)

The [Karak Castle](/wiki/Kerak_Castle "Kerak Castle") (c. 12th century) built by the [Crusaders](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Jerusalem "Kingdom of Jerusalem"), and later expanded under the Muslim [Ayyubids](/wiki/Ayyubids "Ayyubids") and [Mamluks](/wiki/Mamluks "Mamluks")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Ajloun_Castle_1.jpg/250px-Ajloun_Castle_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ajloun_Castle_1.jpg)

The [Ajloun Castle](/wiki/Ajloun_Castle "Ajloun Castle") (c. 12th century) built by the Ayyubid leader [Saladin](/wiki/Saladin "Saladin") for use against the [Crusades](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades")

In 1516 the [Ottoman Caliphate](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire")'s forces [conquered Mamluk territory](/wiki/Ottoman%E2%80%93Mamluk_War_(1516%E2%80%931517) "Ottoman–Mamluk War (1516–1517)").[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199826-68) Agricultural villages in Transjordan witnessed a period of relative prosperity in the 16th century but were later abandoned.[[66]](#cite_note-VSS-69) Transjordan was of marginal importance to the Ottoman authorities.[[67]](#cite_note-atlas10-70) As a result, Ottoman presence was virtually absent and reduced to annual tax collection visits.[[66]](#cite_note-VSS-69)

More Arab [Bedouin](/wiki/Bedouin "Bedouin") tribes moved into Transjordan from Syria and the Hejaz during the first three centuries of Ottoman rule, including the [Adwan](/w/index.php?title=Banu_%27Adwan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Banu 'Adwan (page does not exist)"), the [Bani Sakhr](/wiki/Bani_Sakhr "Bani Sakhr") and the [Howeitat](/wiki/Howeitat "Howeitat").[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199826,_27-71) These tribes laid claims to different parts of the region, and with the absence of a meaningful Ottoman authority, Transjordan slid into a state of anarchy that continued until the 19th century.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199827-72) This led to a short-lived occupation by the [Wahhabi](/wiki/Wahhabism "Wahhabism") forces (1803–1812), an ultra-orthodox Islamic movement that emerged in [Najd](/wiki/Emirate_of_Diriyah "Emirate of Diriyah") (in modern-day Saudi Arabia).[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199830-73) [Ibrahim Pasha](/wiki/Ibrahim_Pasha_of_Egypt "Ibrahim Pasha of Egypt"), son of the [governor](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_of_Egypt "Muhammad Ali of Egypt") of the [Egypt Eyalet](/wiki/Egypt_Eyalet "Egypt Eyalet"), [rooted out the Wahhabis](/wiki/Wahhabi_War "Wahhabi War") under the request of the Ottoman sultan by 1818.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199831-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Z_Ottoman_Ten_arches_Amman_2.jpg/250px-Z_Ottoman_Ten_arches_Amman_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Z_Ottoman_Ten_arches_Amman_2.jpg)

Ottoman [Ten Arches Bridge](/wiki/Ten_Arches_Bridge "Ten Arches Bridge") built south of Amman in 1904, as part of the [Hejaz railway](/wiki/Hejaz_railway "Hejaz railway"), which stretched across the length of Transjordan and linked [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus") with [Medina](/wiki/Medina "Medina")

In 1833 Pasha turned on the Ottomans and established his rule over the Levant.[[72]](#cite_note-cup-75) His policies led to the unsuccessful [peasants' revolt in Palestine](/wiki/Peasants%27_revolt_in_Palestine "Peasants' revolt in Palestine") in 1834.[[72]](#cite_note-cup-75) Transjordanian cities of [As-Salt](/wiki/As-Salt "As-Salt") and Al-Karak [were destroyed](/wiki/Siege_of_Al-Karak_(1834) "Siege of Al-Karak (1834)") by Pasha's forces for harboring a [peasants' revolt leader](/wiki/Qasim_al-Ahmad "Qasim al-Ahmad").[[72]](#cite_note-cup-75) Egyptian rule was [forcibly ended](/wiki/Egyptian%E2%80%93Ottoman_War_(1839%E2%80%9341) "Egyptian–Ottoman War (1839–41)") in 1841, with Ottoman rule restored.[[72]](#cite_note-cup-75) Only after Pasha's campaign did the Ottoman Empire try to solidify its presence in the [Syria Vilayet](/wiki/Syria_Vilayet "Syria Vilayet"), which Transjordan was part of.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199837-76)

A series of tax and land reforms (*[Tanzimat](/wiki/Tanzimat "Tanzimat")*) in 1864 brought some prosperity back to agriculture and to abandoned villages; the end of virtual autonomy led a backlash in other areas of Transjordan.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199837-76) Muslim [Circassians](/wiki/Circassians "Circassians") and [Chechens](/wiki/Chechens "Chechens"), fleeing [Russian persecution](/wiki/Circassian_genocide "Circassian genocide"), sought refuge in the Levant.[[74]](#cite_note-leg-77) In Transjordan and with Ottoman support, Circassians first settled in the long-abandoned vicinity of Amman in 1867 and later in the surrounding villages.[[74]](#cite_note-leg-77) The Ottoman authorities' establishment of its administration, conscription and heavy taxation policies led to revolts in the areas it controlled.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199838-78) Transjordan's tribes in particular revolted during the [Shoubak](/wiki/Shoubak_revolts "Shoubak revolts") (1905) and the [Karak revolts](/wiki/Karak_revolt "Karak revolt") (1910), which were brutally suppressed.[[74]](#cite_note-leg-77) The construction of the [Hejaz Railway](/wiki/Hejaz_Railway "Hejaz Railway") in 1908—stretching across the length of Transjordan and linking [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus") with [Medina](/wiki/Medina "Medina")—helped the population economically, as Transjordan became a stopover for pilgrims.[[74]](#cite_note-leg-77)

### Modern era

Main article: [Emirate of Transjordan](/wiki/Emirate_of_Transjordan "Emirate of Transjordan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/030Arab_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-030Arab_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:030Arab_(cropped).jpg)

Soldiers of the Hashemite-led [Arab Army](/wiki/Arab_Army "Arab Army") holding [the flag](/wiki/Flag_of_the_Arab_Revolt "Flag of the Arab Revolt") of the [Great Arab Revolt](/wiki/Arab_Revolt "Arab Revolt") against the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") in the [Hejaz](/wiki/Hejaz "Hejaz"), 1916

Increasing policies of [Turkification](/wiki/Turkification "Turkification") and centralisation adopted by the Ottoman Empire in the wake of the 1908 [Young Turk Revolution](/wiki/Young_Turk_Revolution "Young Turk Revolution") disenchanted the Arabs of the Levant, which contributed to the development of an [Arab nationalist](/wiki/Arab_nationalist "Arab nationalist") movement. These changes led to the outbreak of the 1916 [Arab Revolt](/wiki/Arab_Revolt "Arab Revolt") during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), which ended four centuries of stagnation under Ottoman rule.[[74]](#cite_note-leg-77) The revolt was led by [Sharif Hussein](/wiki/Hussein_bin_Ali,_King_of_Hejaz "Hussein bin Ali, King of Hejaz") of Mecca, scion of the [Hashemite family](/wiki/Hashemites "Hashemites") of the Hejaz, and his sons [Abdullah](/wiki/Abdullah_I_of_Jordan "Abdullah I of Jordan"), [Faisal](/wiki/Faisal_I_of_Iraq "Faisal I of Iraq") and [Ali](/wiki/Ali_bin_Hussein,_King_of_Hejaz "Ali bin Hussein, King of Hejaz"). Locally, the revolt garnered the support of the Transjordanian tribes, including Bedouins, Circassians and [Christians](/wiki/Jordanian_Christians "Jordanian Christians").[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199841-79) The [Allies of World War I](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I"), including Britain and France whose imperial interests converged with the Arabist cause, offered support.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199840-80) The revolt started on 5 June 1916 from Medina and pushed northwards until the fighting reached Transjordan in the [Battle of Aqaba](/wiki/Battle_of_Aqaba "Battle of Aqaba") on 6 July 1917.[[78]](#cite_note-revolt-81) The revolt reached its climax when Faisal entered Damascus in October 1918 and established an Arab-led military administration in [OETA East](/wiki/Occupied_Enemy_Territory_Administration "Occupied Enemy Territory Administration"), later declared as the [Arab Kingdom of Syria](/wiki/Arab_Kingdom_of_Syria "Arab Kingdom of Syria"), both of which Transjordan was part of.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199841-79) During this period, the southernmost region of the country, including [Ma'an](/wiki/Ma%27an "Ma'an") and [Aqaba](/wiki/Aqaba "Aqaba"), was also [claimed by](/wiki/Occupation_of_Ma%27an "Occupation of Ma'an") the neighbouring [Kingdom of Hejaz](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hejaz "Kingdom of Hejaz").

The nascent Hashemite Kingdom over the [region of Syria](/wiki/Syria_(region) "Syria (region)") was forced to surrender to French troops on 24 July 1920 during the [Battle of Maysalun](/wiki/Battle_of_Maysalun "Battle of Maysalun");[[79]](#cite_note-82) the French occupied only the northern part of Syria, leaving [Transjordan in a period of interregnum](/wiki/Interregnum_(Transjordan) "Interregnum (Transjordan)"). Arab aspirations failed to gain international recognition, due mainly to the secret 1916 [Sykes–Picot Agreement](/wiki/Sykes%E2%80%93Picot_Agreement "Sykes–Picot Agreement"), which divided the region into French and British spheres of influence, and the 1917 [Balfour Declaration](/wiki/Balfour_Declaration "Balfour Declaration"), in which Britain announced its support for the establishment of a "national home" for Jews in Palestine.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199834-83) This was seen by the Hashemites and the Arabs as a betrayal of their previous agreements with the British,[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199871-84) including the 1915 [McMahon–Hussein Correspondence](/wiki/McMahon%E2%80%93Hussein_Correspondence "McMahon–Hussein Correspondence"), in which the British stated their willingness to recognise the independence of a unified Arab state stretching from [Aleppo](/wiki/Aleppo "Aleppo") to [Aden](/wiki/Aden "Aden") under the rule of the Hashemites.[[82]](#cite_note-85)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/The_high_commissioner%27s_first_visit_to_Transjordan%2C_in_Es-Salt..jpg/250px-The_high_commissioner%27s_first_visit_to_Transjordan%2C_in_Es-Salt..jpg)](/wiki/File:The_high_commissioner%27s_first_visit_to_Transjordan,_in_Es-Salt..jpg)

[As-Salt](/wiki/As-Salt "As-Salt") residents gather on 20 August 1920 during the [British](/wiki/Mandate_for_Palestine "Mandate for Palestine") [High Commissioner](/wiki/High_Commissioners_for_Palestine_and_Transjordan "High Commissioners for Palestine and Transjordan")'s visit to Transjordan.

British [High Commissioner](/wiki/List_of_high_commissioners_for_Palestine_and_Transjordan "List of high commissioners for Palestine and Transjordan") [Herbert Samuel](/wiki/Herbert_Samuel,_1st_Viscount_Samuel "Herbert Samuel, 1st Viscount Samuel") travelled to Transjordan on 21 August 1920 to meet with As-Salt's residents. He there declared to a crowd of 600 Transjordanian notables that the British government would aid the establishment of local governments in Transjordan, which was to be kept separate from that of Palestine. The second meeting took place in [Umm Qais](/wiki/Umm_Qais "Umm Qais") on 2 September, where the British representative Major [Fitzroy Somerset](/wiki/FitzRoy_Somerset,_4th_Baron_Raglan "FitzRoy Somerset, 4th Baron Raglan") received a petition that demanded: an independent Arab government in Transjordan to be led by an Arab prince (emir); land sale in Transjordan to [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews") be stopped as well as the prevention of Jewish immigration there; that Britain establish and fund a national army; and that free trade be maintained between Transjordan and the rest of the region.[[83]](#cite_note-tj-86)

Abdullah, the second son of Sharif Hussein, [arrived from Hejaz by train in Ma'an](/wiki/Establishment_of_the_Emirate_of_Transjordan "Establishment of the Emirate of Transjordan") in southern Transjordan on 21 November 1920 to redeem the Greater Syrian Kingdom his brother had lost.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199882-87) Transjordan then was in disarray, widely considered to be ungovernable with its dysfunctional local governments.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199891-88) Abdullah gained the trust of Transjordan's tribal leaders before scrambling to convince them of the benefits of an organised government.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi1998100-89) Abdullah's successes drew the envy of the British, even when it was in their interest.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi1998101-90) The British reluctantly accepted Abdullah as ruler of Transjordan after having given him a six-month trial.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199896-91) In March 1921, the British decided to add Transjordan to their [Mandate for Palestine](/wiki/Mandate_for_Palestine "Mandate for Palestine"), in which they would implement their "[Sharifian Solution](/wiki/Sharifian_Solution "Sharifian Solution")" policy without applying the provisions of the mandate dealing with Jewish settlement. On 11 April 1921 the [Emirate of Transjordan](/wiki/Emirate_of_Transjordan "Emirate of Transjordan") was established with Abdullah as emir.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199893-92)

In September 1922, the Council of the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") recognised Transjordan as a state under the terms of the [Transjordan memorandum](/wiki/Transjordan_memorandum "Transjordan memorandum").[[90]](#cite_note-93)[[91]](#cite_note-94) Transjordan remained a British mandate until 1946, but it had been granted a greater level of autonomy than the region west of the Jordan River.[[92]](#cite_note-protectorate-95) Multiple difficulties emerged upon the assumption of power in the region by the Hashemite leadership.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199810,_30,_31,_49,_104-96) In Transjordan, small local [rebellions at Kura](/wiki/Kura_rebellion "Kura rebellion") in 1921 and 1923 were suppressed by Abdullah's forces with the help of the British.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199810,_30,_31,_49,_104-96) Wahhabis from Najd regained strength and [repeatedly raided](/wiki/Ikhwan_raids_on_Transjordan "Ikhwan raids on Transjordan") the southern parts of his territory, seriously threatening the emir's position.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199810,_30,_31,_49,_104-96) The emir was unable to repel those raids without the aid of the local Bedouin tribes and the British, who maintained a military base with a small [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force") detachment close to Amman.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199810,_30,_31,_49,_104-96)

### Post-independence

Main article: [Timeline of the Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan](/wiki/Timeline_of_the_Hashemite_Kingdom_of_Jordan "Timeline of the Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/King_Abdullah_I_of_Jordan_declaring_independence%2C_25_May_1946.jpg/250px-King_Abdullah_I_of_Jordan_declaring_independence%2C_25_May_1946.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Abdullah_I_of_Jordan_declaring_independence,_25_May_1946.jpg)

[King Abdullah I](/wiki/Abdullah_I_of_Jordan "Abdullah I of Jordan") on 25 May 1946 declaring independence, with Prime Minister [Ibrahim Hashem](/wiki/Ibrahim_Hashem "Ibrahim Hashem") in the background

The [Treaty of London](/wiki/Treaty_of_London_(1946) "Treaty of London (1946)"), signed by the British government and the Emir of Transjordan on 22 March 1946, recognised the independence of the state.[[94]](#cite_note-FRUS1946-97) On 25 May 1946, the day that the treaty was ratified by the Transjordan parliament, Transjordan was raised to the status of a kingdom under the name of the *Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan* in Arabic, with Abdullah as its first king; although it continued to be referred to as the Hashemite Kingdom of Transjordan in English until 1949.[[95]](#cite_note-98)[[96]](#cite_note-99) 25 May is now celebrated as the nation's [Independence Day](/wiki/Independence_Day_(Jordan) "Independence Day (Jordan)"), a [public holiday](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Jordan "Public holidays in Jordan").[[97]](#cite_note-100) Jordan became a member of the United Nations on 14 December 1955.[[98]](#cite_note-HKJ-101)

On 15 May 1948, as part of the [1948 Palestine war](/wiki/1948_Palestine_war "1948 Palestine war"), Jordan intervened along with several other Arab states.[[99]](#cite_note-102) Following the war, Jordan controlled the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank"), and on 24 April 1950 Jordan formally [annexed these territories](/wiki/Jordanian_annexation_of_the_West_Bank "Jordanian annexation of the West Bank") after the [Jericho Conference](/wiki/Jericho_Conference "Jericho Conference").[[100]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-103)[[101]](#cite_note-104) In response, some Arab countries demanded Jordan's expulsion from the [Arab League](/wiki/Arab_League "Arab League").[[100]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-103) On 12 June 1950, the Arab League declared that the annexation was a temporary, practical measure and that Jordan was holding the territory as a "trustee" pending a future settlement.[[102]](#cite_note-105)

King Abdullah was assassinated at the [Al-Aqsa Mosque](/wiki/Al-Aqsa_Mosque "Al-Aqsa Mosque") in 1951 by a Palestinian militant, amid rumors he intended to sign a peace treaty with Israel.[[103]](#cite_note-kaiofh-106) Abdullah was succeeded by his son [Talal](/wiki/Talal_of_Jordan "Talal of Jordan"), who established the [country's modern constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Jordan "Constitution of Jordan") in 1952.[[104]](#cite_note-kojt-107) Illness caused Talal to abdicate to his eldest son [Hussein](/wiki/Hussein_of_Jordan "Hussein of Jordan"),[[104]](#cite_note-kojt-107) who ascended to the throne in 1953 at age 17.[[103]](#cite_note-kaiofh-106) Jordan witnessed great political uncertainty in the following period.[[105]](#cite_note-poinjk-108) The 1950s was a period of political upheaval, as [Nasserism](/wiki/Nasserism "Nasserism") and [Pan-Arabism](/wiki/Pan-Arabism "Pan-Arabism") swept the Arab World.[[105]](#cite_note-poinjk-108) On 1 March 1956, King Hussein [Arabised the command of the Army](/wiki/Arabization_of_the_Jordanian_Army_command "Arabization of the Jordanian Army command") by dismissing a number of senior British officers, an act made to remove remaining foreign influence in the country.[[106]](#cite_note-109) In 1958, Jordan and neighbouring [Hashemite Iraq](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Iraq "Kingdom of Iraq") formed the [Arab Federation](/wiki/Arab_Federation "Arab Federation") as a response to the formation of the rival [United Arab Republic](/wiki/United_Arab_Republic "United Arab Republic") between Nasser's Egypt and Syria.[[107]](#cite_note-jejok-110) The union lasted only six months, being dissolved after Iraqi King [Faisal II](/wiki/Faisal_II "Faisal II") (Hussein's cousin) was deposed by a bloody [military coup on 14 July 1958](/wiki/14_July_Revolution "14 July Revolution").[[107]](#cite_note-jejok-110)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Karama_aftermath_1.jpg/250px-Karama_aftermath_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karama_aftermath_1.jpg)

[King Hussein](/wiki/Hussein_of_Jordan "Hussein of Jordan") on 21 March 1968 checking an abandoned [Israeli](/wiki/Israel "Israel") tank in the aftermath of the [Battle of Karameh](/wiki/Battle_of_Karameh "Battle of Karameh")

Jordan signed a military pact with Egypt just before Israel launched a preemptive strike on Egypt to begin the [Six-Day War](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War") in June 1967, where Jordan and Syria joined the war.[[108]](#cite_note-jowars-111) The Arab states were defeated, and Jordan lost control of the West Bank to Israel.[[108]](#cite_note-jowars-111) The [War of Attrition](/wiki/War_of_Attrition "War of Attrition") with Israel followed, which included the 1968 [Battle of Karameh](/wiki/Battle_of_Karameh "Battle of Karameh") where the combined forces of the [Jordanian Armed Forces](/wiki/Jordanian_Armed_Forces "Jordanian Armed Forces") and the [Palestine Liberation Organisation](/wiki/Palestine_Liberation_Organization "Palestine Liberation Organization") (PLO) repelled an Israeli attack on the [Karameh](/wiki/Karameh "Karameh") camp on the Jordanian border with the West Bank.[[108]](#cite_note-jowars-111) Despite the fact that the Palestinians had limited involvement against the Israeli forces, the events at Karameh gained wide recognition and acclaim in the Arab world.[[109]](#cite_note-Muzaffar_Husain_Syed,_Syed_Saud_Akhtar,_B_D_Usmani-112) As a result, there was an upsurge of support for Palestinian paramilitary elements (the [fedayeen](/wiki/Palestinian_fedayeen "Palestinian fedayeen")) within Jordan from other Arab countries.[[109]](#cite_note-Muzaffar_Husain_Syed,_Syed_Saud_Akhtar,_B_D_Usmani-112) The fedayeen activities soon became a threat to Jordan's rule of law.[[109]](#cite_note-Muzaffar_Husain_Syed,_Syed_Saud_Akhtar,_B_D_Usmani-112) In September 1970, the Jordanian army targeted the fedayeen and the resultant fighting led to the expulsion of Palestinian fighters from various PLO groups into Lebanon, in a conflict that became known as [Black September](/wiki/Black_September "Black September").[[109]](#cite_note-Muzaffar_Husain_Syed,_Syed_Saud_Akhtar,_B_D_Usmani-112)

In 1973, Egypt and Syria waged the [Yom Kippur War](/wiki/Yom_Kippur_War "Yom Kippur War") on Israel, and fighting occurred along the 1967 [Jordan River](/wiki/Jordan_River "Jordan River") cease-fire line.[[109]](#cite_note-Muzaffar_Husain_Syed,_Syed_Saud_Akhtar,_B_D_Usmani-112) Jordan sent a brigade to Syria to attack Israeli units on Syrian territory but did not engage Israeli forces from Jordanian territory.[[109]](#cite_note-Muzaffar_Husain_Syed,_Syed_Saud_Akhtar,_B_D_Usmani-112) At the [Rabat summit conference](/wiki/1974_Arab_League_summit "1974 Arab League summit") in 1974, in the aftermath of the Yom-Kippur War, Jordan and the rest of the Arab League agreed that the PLO was the "sole legitimate representative of the Palestinian people".[[109]](#cite_note-Muzaffar_Husain_Syed,_Syed_Saud_Akhtar,_B_D_Usmani-112) Subsequently, Jordan [renounced](/wiki/Jordanian_disengagement_from_the_West_Bank "Jordanian disengagement from the West Bank") its claims to the West Bank in 1988.[[109]](#cite_note-Muzaffar_Husain_Syed,_Syed_Saud_Akhtar,_B_D_Usmani-112)

At the [1991 Madrid Conference](/wiki/Madrid_Conference_of_1991 "Madrid Conference of 1991"), Jordan agreed to negotiate a peace treaty sponsored by the US and the Soviet Union.[[109]](#cite_note-Muzaffar_Husain_Syed,_Syed_Saud_Akhtar,_B_D_Usmani-112) The [Israel–Jordan peace treaty](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93Jordan_peace_treaty "Israel–Jordan peace treaty") was signed on 26 October 1994.[[109]](#cite_note-Muzaffar_Husain_Syed,_Syed_Saud_Akhtar,_B_D_Usmani-112) In 1997, in retribution for [a bombing](/wiki/1997_Mahane_Yehuda_Market_bombings "1997 Mahane Yehuda Market bombings"), Israeli agents entered Jordan using Canadian passports and poisoned [Khaled Mashal](/wiki/Khaled_Mashal "Khaled Mashal"), a senior [Hamas](/wiki/Hamas "Hamas") leader living in Jordan.[[109]](#cite_note-Muzaffar_Husain_Syed,_Syed_Saud_Akhtar,_B_D_Usmani-112) Bowing to intense international pressure, Israel provided an antidote to the poison and released dozens of political prisoners, including [Sheikh Ahmed Yassin](/wiki/Sheikh_Ahmed_Yassin "Sheikh Ahmed Yassin"), after King Hussein threatened to annul the peace treaty.[[109]](#cite_note-Muzaffar_Husain_Syed,_Syed_Saud_Akhtar,_B_D_Usmani-112)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/HabisAlMajali%26WasfiAlTall.jpg/250px-HabisAlMajali%26WasfiAlTall.jpg)](/wiki/File:HabisAlMajali%26WasfiAlTall.jpg)

Army Chief [Habis Majali](/wiki/Habis_Majali "Habis Majali") and Prime Minister [Wasfi Tal](/wiki/Wasfi_Tal "Wasfi Tal") during a military parade in 1970, two widely acclaimed national figures

On 7 February 1999, [Abdullah II](/wiki/Abdullah_II_of_Jordan "Abdullah II of Jordan") ascended the throne upon the death of his father Hussein, who had ruled for nearly 50 years.[[110]](#cite_note-113) Abdullah embarked on economic liberalisation when he assumed the throne, and his reforms led to an economic boom which continued until 2008.[[111]](#cite_note-IMFjohk-114) Abdullah II has been credited with increasing foreign investment, improving public-private partnerships and providing the foundation for [Aqaba](/wiki/Aqaba "Aqaba")'s free-trade zone and Jordan's flourishing information and communication technology sector.[[111]](#cite_note-IMFjohk-114) He also set up five other special economic zones.[[111]](#cite_note-IMFjohk-114) However, during the following years Jordan's economy experienced hardship as it dealt with the effects of the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession") and spillover from the [Arab Spring](/wiki/Arab_Spring "Arab Spring").[[112]](#cite_note-instability1-115)

[Al-Qaeda](/wiki/Al-Qaeda "Al-Qaeda") under [Abu Musab al-Zarqawi](/wiki/Abu_Musab_al-Zarqawi "Abu Musab al-Zarqawi")'s leadership launched [coordinated explosions](/wiki/2005_Amman_bombings "2005 Amman bombings") in three hotel lobbies in Amman on 9 November 2005, resulting in 60 deaths and 115 injured.[[113]](#cite_note-acbsya-116) The bombings, which targeted civilians, caused widespread outrage among Jordanians.[[113]](#cite_note-acbsya-116) The attack is considered to be a rare event in the country, and Jordan's [internal security](/wiki/General_Intelligence_Directorate_(Jordan) "General Intelligence Directorate (Jordan)") was dramatically improved afterwards.[[113]](#cite_note-acbsya-116) No major terrorist attacks have occurred since then.[[114]](#cite_note-117) Abdullah and Jordan are viewed with contempt by Islamic extremists for the country's peace treaty with Israel, its relationship with the West, and its mostly non-religious laws.[[115]](#cite_note-ahbbnyt-118)

The Arab Spring were large-scale protests that erupted in the [Arab world](/wiki/Arab_world "Arab world") in 2011, demanding economic and political reforms.[[116]](#cite_note-jkfchh-119) Many of these protests tore down regimes in some Arab nations, leading to instability that ended with violent civil wars.[[116]](#cite_note-jkfchh-119) In response to [domestic unrest](/wiki/2011%E2%80%932012_Jordanian_protests "2011–2012 Jordanian protests"), Abdullah replaced his prime minister and introduced reforms including reforming the constitution and laws governing public freedoms and elections.[[116]](#cite_note-jkfchh-119) Proportional representation was re-introduced to the Jordanian parliament in the [2016 general election](/wiki/2016_Jordanian_general_election "2016 Jordanian general election"), a move which he said would eventually lead to establishing parliamentary governments.[[117]](#cite_note-hhjdrp-120) Jordan was left largely unscathed from the violence that swept the region despite an influx of 1.4 million Syrian refugees into the natural resources-lacking country and the emergence of the [Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant](/wiki/Islamic_State_of_Iraq_and_the_Levant "Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant") (ISIL).[[117]](#cite_note-hhjdrp-120)

On 4 April 2021, [19 people were arrested](/wiki/2021_arrests_in_Jordan "2021 arrests in Jordan"), including [Prince Hamzeh](/wiki/Prince_Hamzah_bin_Hussein "Prince Hamzah bin Hussein"), the former crown prince of Jordan, who was placed under house arrest, after having been accused of working to "destabilize" the kingdom.

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Jordan](/wiki/Geography_of_Jordan "Geography of Jordan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Mountain_in_Wadi_Rum%2C_Jordan.jpg/250px-Mountain_in_Wadi_Rum%2C_Jordan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mountain_in_Wadi_Rum,_Jordan.jpg)

[Wadi Rum](/wiki/Wadi_Rum "Wadi Rum") is a valley in southern Jordan whose resemblance to the surface of [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") has made it a popular tourist and filming location, including scenes of [*The Martian*](/wiki/The_Martian_(film) "The Martian (film)").

Jordan sits strategically at the crossroads of the continents of Asia, Africa and Europe,[[118]](#cite_note-mattel-121) in the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant") area of the [Fertile Crescent](/wiki/Fertile_Crescent "Fertile Crescent"), a [cradle of civilisation](/wiki/Cradle_of_civilization "Cradle of civilization").[[119]](#cite_note-geo-122) Its area is 89,341 square kilometres (34,495 sq mi), and it is 400 kilometres (250 mi) long between its northernmost and southernmost points; [Umm Qais](/wiki/Umm_Qais "Umm Qais") and [Aqaba](/wiki/Aqaba "Aqaba") respectively.[[14]](#cite_note-CIA_factbook-17) The kingdom lies between [29°](/wiki/29th_parallel_north "29th parallel north") and [34° N](/wiki/34th_parallel_north "34th parallel north"), and [34°](/wiki/34th_meridian_east "34th meridian east") and [40° E](/wiki/40th_meridian_east "40th meridian east"). It is bordered by [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") to [the south](/wiki/Jordan%E2%80%93Saudi_Arabia_border "Jordan–Saudi Arabia border") and the east, [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq") to [the north-east](/wiki/Iraq%E2%80%93Jordan_border "Iraq–Jordan border"), [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria") to [the north](/wiki/Jordan%E2%80%93Syria_border "Jordan–Syria border"), and [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") and [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine "Palestine") ([West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank")) to the west.

The east is an arid plateau irrigated by [oases](/wiki/Oasis "Oasis") and seasonal streams.[[14]](#cite_note-CIA_factbook-17) Major cities are overwhelmingly located on the north-western part of the kingdom with its fertile soils and relatively abundant rainfall.[[120]](#cite_note-gbdss-123) These include [Irbid](/wiki/Irbid "Irbid"), [Jerash](/wiki/Jerash "Jerash") and [Zarqa](/wiki/Zarqa "Zarqa") in the northwest, the capital [Amman](/wiki/Amman "Amman") and [As-Salt](/wiki/As-Salt "As-Salt") in the central west, and [Madaba](/wiki/Madaba "Madaba"), [Al-Karak](/wiki/Al-Karak "Al-Karak") and Aqaba in the southwest.[[120]](#cite_note-gbdss-123) Major towns in the east are the oasis towns of [Azraq](/wiki/Azraq "Azraq") and [Ruwaished](/wiki/Ruwaished "Ruwaished").[[119]](#cite_note-geo-122)

In the west, a [highland area](/wiki/Jordanian_Highlands "Jordanian Highlands") of arable land and Mediterranean evergreen forestry drops suddenly into the [Jordan Rift Valley](/wiki/Jordan_Rift_Valley "Jordan Rift Valley").[[119]](#cite_note-geo-122) The rift valley contains the [Jordan River](/wiki/Jordan_River "Jordan River") and the [Dead Sea](/wiki/Dead_Sea "Dead Sea"), which separates Jordan from Israel.[[119]](#cite_note-geo-122) Jordan has a 26 kilometres (16 mi) shoreline on the [Gulf of Aqaba](/wiki/Gulf_of_Aqaba "Gulf of Aqaba") in the [Red Sea](/wiki/Red_Sea "Red Sea") but is otherwise landlocked.[[121]](#cite_note-Jogeo-124) The [Yarmuk River](/wiki/Yarmuk_(river) "Yarmuk (river)"), an eastern tributary of the Jordan, forms part of the boundary between Jordan and Syria (including the [Israeli-occupied](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_Golan_Heights "Israeli occupation of the Golan Heights") [Golan Heights](/wiki/Golan_Heights "Golan Heights")) to the north.[[121]](#cite_note-Jogeo-124) The other boundaries are formed by several international and local agreements and do not follow well-defined natural features.[[119]](#cite_note-geo-122) The highest point is [Jabal Umm al Dami](/wiki/Jabal_Umm_al_Dami "Jabal Umm al Dami"), at 1,854 m (6,083 ft) above sea level, while the lowest is the Dead Sea −420 m (−1,378 ft), the [lowest land point on Earth](/wiki/Extremes_on_Earth "Extremes on Earth").[[119]](#cite_note-geo-122)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Dead_sea_newspaper.jpg/250px-Dead_sea_newspaper.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dead_sea_newspaper.jpg)

The [Dead Sea](/wiki/Dead_Sea "Dead Sea"), which is located along Jordan's western border, is the lowest point on Earth and a natural resort that has attracted visitors from across the region for millennia.

Jordan has a diverse range of habitats, ecosystems and biota because of its varied landscapes and environments.[[122]](#cite_note-jordan-chm-125) The [Royal Society for the Conservation of Nature](/wiki/Royal_Society_for_the_Conservation_of_Nature "Royal Society for the Conservation of Nature") was set up in 1966 to protect and manage Jordan's natural resources.[[123]](#cite_note-tgjobd-126) [Nature reserves in Jordan](/wiki/Nature_reserves_in_Jordan "Nature reserves in Jordan") include the [Dana Biosphere Reserve](/wiki/Dana_Biosphere_Reserve "Dana Biosphere Reserve"), the [Azraq Wetland Reserve](/wiki/Azraq_Wetland_Reserve "Azraq Wetland Reserve"), the [Shaumari Wildlife Reserve](/wiki/Shaumari_Wildlife_Reserve "Shaumari Wildlife Reserve") and the [Mujib Nature Reserve](/wiki/Wadi_Mujib "Wadi Mujib").[[123]](#cite_note-tgjobd-126)

### Climate

Main articles: [Climate of Jordan](/wiki/Climate_of_Jordan "Climate of Jordan") and [Climate change in Jordan](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Jordan "Climate change in Jordan")

The climate varies greatly; generally, the further inland from the Mediterranean, there are greater contrasts in temperature and less rainfall.[[14]](#cite_note-CIA_factbook-17) The average elevation is 812 m (2,664 ft) above sea level.[[14]](#cite_note-CIA_factbook-17) The highlands above the Jordan Valley, mountains of the Dead Sea and Wadi [Araba](/wiki/Arabah "Arabah") and as far south as Ras Al-Naqab are dominated by a [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate"), while the eastern and northeastern areas of the country are arid desert.[[124]](#cite_note-majocw-127) Although the deserts reach high temperatures, the heat is usually moderated by low humidity and a daytime breeze, while the nights are cool.[[125]](#cite_note-obgjo-128)

Summers, lasting from May to September, are hot and dry, with temperatures averaging around 32 °C (90 °F) and sometimes exceeding 40 °C (104 °F) between July and August.[[125]](#cite_note-obgjo-128) The winter, lasting from November to March, is relatively cool, with temperatures averaging around 11.08 °C (52 °F).[[124]](#cite_note-majocw-127) Winter also sees frequent showers and occasional snowfall in some western elevated areas.[[124]](#cite_note-majocw-127)

### Biodiversity

Main article: [Wildlife of Jordan](/wiki/Wildlife_of_Jordan "Wildlife of Jordan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Ajlun_Green.jpg/250px-Ajlun_Green.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ajlun_Green.jpg)

A forest in [Ajloun](/wiki/Ajloun "Ajloun"), northern Jordan. Forests make less than 2% of Jordan, making it among the world's least forested countries.

Over 2,000 plant species have been recorded.[[126]](#cite_note-129) Many of the flowering plants bloom in the spring after the winter rains and the type of vegetation depends largely on the levels of precipitation. The mountainous regions in the northwest are clothed in forests, while further south and east the vegetation becomes more scrubby and transitions to steppe-type vegetation.[[127]](#cite_note-130) Forests cover 1.5 million [dunums](/wiki/Dunam "Dunam") (1,500 km2), less than 2% of Jordan, making Jordan among the world's least forested countries, the international average being 15%.[[128]](#cite_note-131)

Plant species and genera include the [Aleppo pine](/wiki/Pinus_halepensis "Pinus halepensis"), *[Sarcopoterium](/wiki/Sarcopoterium "Sarcopoterium")*, *[Salvia dominica](/wiki/Salvia_dominica "Salvia dominica")*, [black iris](/wiki/Black_iris "Black iris"), *[Tamarix](/wiki/Tamarix "Tamarix")*, *[Anabasis](/wiki/Anabasis_(plant) "Anabasis (plant)")*, *[Artemisia](/wiki/Artemisia_(genus) "Artemisia (genus)")*, *[Acacia](/wiki/Acacia "Acacia")*, [Mediterranean cypress](/wiki/Cupressus_sempervirens "Cupressus sempervirens") and [Phoenecian juniper](/wiki/Juniperus_phoenicea "Juniperus phoenicea").[[129]](#cite_note-132) The mountainous regions in the northwest are clothed in natural forests of [pine](/wiki/Pine "Pine"), [deciduous oak](/wiki/Deciduous "Deciduous"), [evergreen oak](/wiki/Quercus_ilex "Quercus ilex"), [pistachio](/wiki/Pistachio "Pistachio") and wild [olive](/wiki/Olive "Olive").[[130]](#cite_note-Jordan-133) Mammal and reptile species include, the [long-eared hedgehog](/wiki/Long-eared_hedgehog "Long-eared hedgehog"), [Nubian ibex](/wiki/Nubian_ibex "Nubian ibex"), [wild boar](/wiki/Wild_boar "Wild boar"), [fallow deer](/wiki/Fallow_deer "Fallow deer"), [Arabian wolf](/wiki/Arabian_wolf "Arabian wolf"), [desert monitor](/wiki/Desert_monitor "Desert monitor"), [honey badger](/wiki/Honey_badger "Honey badger"), [glass snake](/wiki/Glass_snake "Glass snake"), [caracal](/wiki/Caracal "Caracal"), [golden jackal](/wiki/Golden_jackal "Golden jackal") and the [roe deer](/wiki/Roe_deer "Roe deer"), among others.[[131]](#cite_note-134)[[132]](#cite_note-135)[[133]](#cite_note-Qumsiyeh-136) Bird include the [hooded crow](/wiki/Hooded_crow "Hooded crow"), [Eurasian jay](/wiki/Eurasian_jay "Eurasian jay"), [lappet-faced vulture](/wiki/Lappet-faced_vulture "Lappet-faced vulture"), [barbary falcon](/wiki/Barbary_falcon "Barbary falcon"), [hoopoe](/wiki/Hoopoe "Hoopoe"), [pharaoh eagle-owl](/wiki/Pharaoh_eagle-owl "Pharaoh eagle-owl"), [common cuckoo](/wiki/Common_cuckoo "Common cuckoo"), [Tristram's starling](/wiki/Tristram%27s_starling "Tristram's starling"), [Palestine sunbird](/wiki/Palestine_sunbird "Palestine sunbird"), [Sinai rosefinch](/wiki/Sinai_rosefinch "Sinai rosefinch"), [lesser kestrel](/wiki/Lesser_kestrel "Lesser kestrel"), [house crow](/wiki/House_crow "House crow") and the [white-spectacled bulbul](/wiki/White-spectacled_bulbul "White-spectacled bulbul").[[134]](#cite_note-137)

Four terrestrial ecoregions lie with Jordan's borders: [Syrian xeric grasslands and shrublands](/wiki/Syrian_xeric_grasslands_and_shrublands "Syrian xeric grasslands and shrublands"), [Eastern Mediterranean conifer-sclerophyllous-broadleaf forests](/wiki/Eastern_Mediterranean_conifer-sclerophyllous-broadleaf_forests "Eastern Mediterranean conifer-sclerophyllous-broadleaf forests"), [Mesopotamian shrub desert](/wiki/Mesopotamian_shrub_desert "Mesopotamian shrub desert"), and [Red Sea Nubo-Sindian tropical desert and semi-desert](/wiki/Red_Sea_Nubo-Sindian_tropical_desert_and_semi-desert "Red Sea Nubo-Sindian tropical desert and semi-desert").[[135]](#cite_note-DinersteinOlson2017-138)

## Government and politics

Main article: [Politics of Jordan](/wiki/Politics_of_Jordan "Politics of Jordan")

Jordan is a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") under a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"). Its [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Jordan "Constitution of Jordan"), adopted in 1952 and amended a number of times since, is the legal framework that governs the monarch, government, bicameral legislature and judiciary.[[136]](#cite_note-const-139) The king retains wide executive and legislative powers from the [government](/wiki/Government_of_Jordan "Government of Jordan") and [parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Jordan "Parliament of Jordan").[[137]](#cite_note-140) The king exercises his powers through the government that he appoints for a four-year term, which is responsible before the parliament that is made up of two chambers: the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_(Jordan) "Senate (Jordan)") and the [House of Representatives](/wiki/House_of_Representatives_(Jordan) "House of Representatives (Jordan)"). The judiciary is independent according to the constitution but in practice often lacks independence.[[136]](#cite_note-const-139)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Abdullah_II_portrait_crop_2_at_10_Downing_Street_June_2025.jpg/250px-Abdullah_II_portrait_crop_2_at_10_Downing_Street_June_2025.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abdullah_II_portrait_crop_2_at_10_Downing_Street_June_2025.jpg)

[Abdullah II](/wiki/Abdullah_II_of_Jordan "Abdullah II of Jordan")  
[King](/wiki/List_of_kings_of_Jordan "List of kings of Jordan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/HE_Jafar_Hassan_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-HE_Jafar_Hassan_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:HE_Jafar_Hassan_(cropped).jpg)

[Jafar Hassan](/wiki/Jafar_Hassan "Jafar Hassan")  
[Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Jordan "Prime Minister of Jordan")

The king is the [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") and [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief") of the [Armed Forces](/wiki/Jordanian_Armed_Forces "Jordanian Armed Forces"). He can declare war and peace, ratify laws and treaties, convene and close legislative sessions, call and postpone elections, dismiss the government, and dissolve the parliament.[[136]](#cite_note-const-139) The appointed government can also be dismissed through a majority [vote of no confidence](/wiki/Motion_of_no_confidence "Motion of no confidence") by the elected House of Representatives. After a bill is proposed by the government, it must be approved by the House of Representatives then the Senate and becomes law after being ratified by the king. A royal veto on legislation can be overridden by a [two-thirds vote](/wiki/Supermajority#Two-thirds_vote "Supermajority") in a joint session of both houses. The parliament also has the right of [interpellation](/wiki/Interpellation_(politics) "Interpellation (politics)").[[136]](#cite_note-const-139)

The 65 members of the upper Senate are directly appointed by the king, the constitution mandates that they be veteran politicians, judges and generals who previously served in the government or in the House of Representatives.[[138]](#cite_note-jopseaf-141) The 130 members of the lower House of Representatives are elected through [party-list proportional representation](/wiki/Party-list_proportional_representation "Party-list proportional representation") in 23 constituencies for a 4-year term.[[139]](#cite_note-142) Minimum quotas exist in the House of Representatives for women (15 seats, though they won 20 seats in the 2016 election), Christians (9 seats) and [Circassians](/wiki/Circassians_in_Jordan "Circassians in Jordan") and [Chechens](/wiki/Chechens_in_Jordan "Chechens in Jordan") (3 seats).[[140]](#cite_note-143)

Courts are divided into three categories: civil, religious, and special.[[141]](#cite_note-women-jordan-144) The civil courts deal with civil and criminal matters, including cases brought against the government.[[141]](#cite_note-women-jordan-144) The civil courts include magistrate courts, courts of first instance, courts of appeal,[[141]](#cite_note-women-jordan-144) high administrative courts which hear cases relating to administrative matters,[[142]](#cite_note-145) and the constitutional court which was set up in 2012 in order to hear cases regarding the constitutionality of laws.[[143]](#cite_note-146) Although [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") is the [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion"), the constitution preserves [religious](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion") and personal freedoms. Religious law only extends to matters of personal status such as divorce and inheritance in religious courts, and is partially based on Islamic [sharia](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia") law.[[144]](#cite_note-147) The special court deals with cases forwarded by the civil one.[[145]](#cite_note-unesco1-148)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/%D9%85%D8%AC%D9%84%D8%B3_%D8%A7%D9%84%D9%86%D9%88%D8%A7%D8%A8_%D8%AC%D9%84%D8%B3%D8%A9_16-9-2018_%2812%29.jpg/250px-%D9%85%D8%AC%D9%84%D8%B3_%D8%A7%D9%84%D9%86%D9%88%D8%A7%D8%A8_%D8%AC%D9%84%D8%B3%D8%A9_16-9-2018_%2812%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D9%85%D8%AC%D9%84%D8%B3_%D8%A7%D9%84%D9%86%D9%88%D8%A7%D8%A8_%D8%AC%D9%84%D8%B3%D8%A9_16-9-2018_(12).jpg)

The [House of Representatives](/wiki/Jordanian_House_of_Representatives "Jordanian House of Representatives") during a [parliamentary session](/wiki/Parliament_of_Jordan "Parliament of Jordan")

The monarch, [Abdullah II](/wiki/Abdullah_II_of_Jordan "Abdullah II of Jordan"), ascended to the throne in February 1999 after the death of his father King [Hussein](/wiki/Hussein_of_Jordan "Hussein of Jordan"). Abdullah re-affirmed Jordan's commitment to the [peace treaty](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93Jordan_peace_treaty "Israel–Jordan peace treaty") with [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") and its relations with the United States. He refocused the government's agenda on economic reform during his first year. King Abdullah's eldest son, [Prince Hussein](/wiki/Hussein,_Crown_Prince_of_Jordan "Hussein, Crown Prince of Jordan"), is the Crown Prince of Jordan.[[146]](#cite_note-hamzah-149) The prime minister is [Jafar Hassan](/wiki/Jafar_Hassan "Jafar Hassan") who was appointed on 15 September 2024.[[147]](#cite_note-150) Abdullah had announced his intention to move Jordan to a [parliamentary system](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system"), where the largest bloc in parliament forms a government. However, the underdevelopment of political parties in a country where tribal identity remains strong has hampered the effort.[[148]](#cite_note-151) Jordan has approximately 50 political parties representing nationalist, leftist, Islamist, and liberal ideologies.[[149]](#cite_note-152) Political parties contested one-fifth of the seats in the [2016 elections](/wiki/2016_Jordanian_general_election "2016 Jordanian general election"), the remainder belonging to independent politicians.[[150]](#cite_note-153)

[Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House") ranked Jordan as "Not Free" in the [Freedom in the World](/wiki/Freedom_in_the_World "Freedom in the World") 2022 report.[[151]](#cite_note-154) Jordan ranked 94th globally in the [Cato Institute](/wiki/Cato_Institute "Cato Institute")'s [Human Freedom Index](/wiki/Index_of_Freedom_in_the_World "Index of Freedom in the World") in 2021,[[152]](#cite_note-155) and ranked 58th in the [Corruption Perceptions Index](/wiki/Corruption_Perceptions_Index "Corruption Perceptions Index") issued by [Transparency International](/wiki/Transparency_International "Transparency International") in 2021.[[153]](#cite_note-156) In the 2023 [Press Freedom Index](/wiki/World_Press_Freedom_Index "World Press Freedom Index") by [Reporters Without Borders](/wiki/Reporters_Without_Borders "Reporters Without Borders"), Jordan ranked 146 out of 180 countries. The overall score for Jordan was 42.79, based on a scale from 0 (least free) to 105 (most free). The 2015 report noted "the Arab Spring and the Syrian conflict have led the authorities to tighten their grip on the media and, in particular, the Internet, despite an outcry from civil society".[[154]](#cite_note-157) Jordanian media consists of public and private institutions. Popular Jordanian newspapers include [Al Ghad](/wiki/Al_Ghad "Al Ghad") and the [Jordan Times](/wiki/The_Jordan_Times "The Jordan Times"). [Al-Mamlaka](/wiki/Al-Mamlaka "Al-Mamlaka"), [Roya TV](/wiki/Roya_TV "Roya TV") and [Jordan TV](/wiki/Jordan_Radio_and_Television_Corporation "Jordan Radio and Television Corporation") are some Jordanian television channels.[[155]](#cite_note-158) Internet penetration in Jordan reached 76% in 2015.[[156]](#cite_note-159)

### Administrative divisions

Jordan is divided into 12 [governorates](/wiki/Governorates_of_Jordan "Governorates of Jordan") ([muhafazah](/wiki/Muhafazah "Muhafazah")) informally grouped into three regions: northern, central, southern. The governorates are divided into [*liwa*](/wiki/Liwa_(Arabic) "Liwa (Arabic)") or districts, which are often further subdivided into [*qda*](/wiki/Kaza "Kaza") or sub-districts.[[159]](#cite_note-Muni-162) Control for each administrative unit is in a "chief town" ([administrative centre](/wiki/Administrative_centre "Administrative centre")) known as a [*nahia*](/wiki/Nahias_of_Jordan "Nahias of Jordan").[[159]](#cite_note-Muni-162)
