### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Jordan](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Jordan "Foreign relations of Jordan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Queen_Rania_and_King_Abdullah_II_of_Jordan%2C_Donald_and_Melania_Trump_in_the_Oval_Office%2C_April_2017.jpg/250px-Queen_Rania_and_King_Abdullah_II_of_Jordan%2C_Donald_and_Melania_Trump_in_the_Oval_Office%2C_April_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Rania_and_King_Abdullah_II_of_Jordan,_Donald_and_Melania_Trump_in_the_Oval_Office,_April_2017.jpg)

U.S. President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") and First Lady [Melania Trump](/wiki/Melania_Trump "Melania Trump") with [King Abdullah II](/wiki/King_Abdullah_II "King Abdullah II") and [Queen Rania](/wiki/Queen_Rania "Queen Rania") of Jordan at the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House"), 2017

The kingdom has followed a pro-Western [foreign policy](/wiki/Foreign_policy "Foreign policy") and maintained close relations with the United States and the United Kingdom. During the first [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War") (1990), these relations were damaged by Jordan's neutrality and its maintenance of relations with Iraq. Later, Jordan restored its relations with Western countries through its participation in the enforcement of [UN sanctions against Iraq](/wiki/Sanctions_against_Iraq "Sanctions against Iraq") and in the Southwest Asia peace process. After King Hussein's death in 1999, relations between Jordan and the Persian Gulf countries greatly improved.[[160]](#cite_note-163)

Jordan is a key ally of the US and UK and, together with Egypt and the United Arab Emirates, is one of only three [Arab nations](/wiki/Arab_world "Arab world") to have signed peace treaties with Israel, Jordan's direct neighbour.[[161]](#cite_note-164) Jordan views an independent Palestinian state with the [1967 borders](/wiki/Green_Line_(Israel) "Green Line (Israel)") as part of the [two-state solution](/wiki/Two-state_solution "Two-state solution") and of supreme national interest.[[162]](#cite_note-165) The ruling Hashemite dynasty has had custodianship over holy sites in Jerusalem since 1924, a position reinforced in the Israel–Jordan peace treaty. Turmoil in Jerusalem's [Al-Aqsa](/wiki/Al-Aqsa "Al-Aqsa") mosque between Israelis and Palestinians created tensions between Jordan and Israel concerning the former's role in protecting the Muslim and Christian sites in Jerusalem.[[163]](#cite_note-166)

Jordan is a founding member of the [Organisation of Islamic Cooperation](/wiki/Organisation_of_Islamic_Cooperation "Organisation of Islamic Cooperation") and of the [Arab League](/wiki/Arab_League "Arab League").[[164]](#cite_note-ALO-167)[[165]](#cite_note-168) It enjoys "advanced status" with the European Union and is part of the [European Neighbourhood Policy](/wiki/European_Neighbourhood_Policy "European Neighbourhood Policy"), which aims to increase links between the EU and its neighbours.[[166]](#cite_note-EU-169) Jordan and [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco") tried to join the [Gulf Cooperation Council](/wiki/Gulf_Cooperation_Council "Gulf Cooperation Council") in 2011, but the Gulf countries offered a five-year development aid programme instead.[[167]](#cite_note-170)

### Military

Main article: [Jordanian Armed Forces](/wiki/Jordanian_Armed_Forces "Jordanian Armed Forces")

The first organised army in Jordan was established on 22 October 1920, named the "[Arab Legion](/wiki/Arab_Legion "Arab Legion")".[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalibi199810,_30,_31,_49,_104-96) The Arab Legion grew from 150 men in 1920 to 8,000 in 1946.[[168]](#cite_note-encmew-171) Jordan's capture of the West Bank during the 1948 Arab–Israeli War proved that the Arab Legion, known today as the Jordan Armed Forces, was the most effective among the Arab troops involved in the war.[[168]](#cite_note-encmew-171) The [Royal Jordanian Army](/wiki/Royal_Jordanian_Army "Royal Jordanian Army"), which boasts around 110,000 personnel, is considered to be among the most professional in the region and is particularly well-trained and organised.[[168]](#cite_note-encmew-171) The Jordanian military enjoys strong support and aid from the United States, the United Kingdom and France. This is due to Jordan's critical position in the Middle East.[[168]](#cite_note-encmew-171) The development of [Special Operations Forces](/wiki/Special_Operation_Forces_(Jordan) "Special Operation Forces (Jordan)") has been particularly significant, enhancing the capability of the military to react rapidly to threats to homeland security, as well as training special forces from the region and beyond.[[169]](#cite_note-172) Jordan provides extensive training to the security forces of several Arab countries.[[170]](#cite_note-173)

As of October 2025, there were 237 Jordanian personnel, mostly military and police, working on United Nations peacekeeping missions.[[171]](#cite_note-174) This was a steep drop from earlier years; for example at the end of 2013 the count was 3,254.[[172]](#cite_note-175) Jordan has dispatched several field hospitals to conflict zones and areas affected by natural disasters across the region.[[173]](#cite_note-176)

In 2014, Jordan joined an aerial bombardment campaign by an international coalition led by the United States against the [Islamic State](/wiki/Islamic_State_of_Iraq_and_the_Levant "Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant") as part of its intervention in the [Syrian Civil War](/wiki/Syrian_Civil_War "Syrian Civil War").[[174]](#cite_note-177) In 2015, Jordan participated in the [Saudi Arabian-led military intervention in Yemen](/wiki/Saudi_Arabian-led_intervention_in_Yemen "Saudi Arabian-led intervention in Yemen") against the [Houthis](/wiki/Houthis "Houthis") and forces loyal to former President [Ali Abdullah Saleh](/wiki/Ali_Abdullah_Saleh "Ali Abdullah Saleh"), who was deposed in the 2011 uprising.[[175]](#cite_note-178)

### Law enforcement

Main articles: [Law enforcement in Jordan](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Jordan "Law enforcement in Jordan") and [Law of Jordan](/wiki/Law_of_Jordan "Law of Jordan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Jordanian_Police_automobile_%28Audi%29.JPG/250px-Jordanian_Police_automobile_%28Audi%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Jordanian_Police_automobile_(Audi).JPG)

An [Amman](/wiki/Amman "Amman") City Centre Police patrol vehicle. Jordan's law enforcement was ranked 37th globally by the 2016 World Internal Security and Police Index.

Law enforcement is under the purview of the [Public Security Directorate](/wiki/Public_Security_Directorate "Public Security Directorate") (which includes approximately 50,000 persons) and the [General Directorate of Gendarmerie](/wiki/General_Directorate_of_Gendarmerie "General Directorate of Gendarmerie"), both of which are subordinate to the [Ministry of Interior](/wiki/Ministry_of_Interior_(Jordan) "Ministry of Interior (Jordan)"). The first police force was organised after the fall of the Ottoman Empire on 11 April 1921.[[176]](#cite_note-PSD-179) Until [1956](/wiki/Arabization_of_the_Jordanian_Army_command "Arabization of the Jordanian Army command") police duties were carried out by the Arab Legion and the [Transjordan Frontier Force](/wiki/Transjordan_Frontier_Force "Transjordan Frontier Force"). After that year the Public Safety Directorate was established.[[176]](#cite_note-PSD-179) The number of female police officers is increasing. In the 1970s, it was the first Arab country to include women in its police force.[[177]](#cite_note-180) Jordan's law enforcement was ranked 37th in the world and 3rd in the Middle East, in terms of police services' performance, by the 2016 World Internal Security and Police Index.[[178]](#cite_note-TDBC-181)[[179]](#cite_note-182)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Jordan](/wiki/Economy_of_Jordan "Economy of Jordan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/GDP_per_capita_development_of_Jordan.svg/250px-GDP_per_capita_development_of_Jordan.svg.png)](/wiki/File:GDP_per_capita_development_of_Jordan.svg)

Change in per capita GDP of Jordan, 1950–2018. Figures are inflation-adjusted to 2011 International Geary-Khamis dollars.

Jordan is classified by the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") as a lower middle income country.[[180]](#cite_note-data.worldbank.org-183) The World Food Programme classified Jordan's economy as upper-middle income, as well as being poor in resources with limited land for agriculture.[[181]](#cite_note-184) Approximately 15.7% of the population lived on or below the national poverty line in 2018,[[182]](#cite_note-185) while almost a third fell below the national poverty line during some time of the year, known as [transient poverty](/wiki/Transient_poverty "Transient poverty").[[183]](#cite_note-186) The economy, which has a GDP of $39.453 billion (as of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Jordan&action=edit)),[[5]](#cite_note-IMFWEO.JO-5) grew at an average rate of 8% per annum between 2004 and 2008, and around 2.6% from 2010 onwards.[[14]](#cite_note-CIA_factbook-17) GDP per capita increased by 351% in the 1970s, declined 30% in the 1980s, and rose 36% in the 1990s; it was $9,406 per capita by [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") in 2017.[[184]](#cite_note-187) The Jordanian economy is one of the smallest economies in the region, and the country's populace suffers from relatively high rates of unemployment and poverty.[[14]](#cite_note-CIA_factbook-17)

The economy is relatively well diversified. Trade and finance combined account for nearly one-third of GDP; transportation and communication, public utilities, and construction account for one-fifth, and mining and manufacturing constitute nearly another fifth.[[13]](#cite_note-washington-16) Net [official development assistance](/wiki/Official_development_assistance "Official development assistance") to Jordan in 2009 totalled US$761 million; according to the government, approximately two-thirds of this was allocated as grants, of which half was direct budget support.[[185]](#cite_note-oecd-jord-188)

The official currency is the [Jordanian dinar](/wiki/Jordanian_dinar "Jordanian dinar"), which is pegged to the International Monetary Fund's [special drawing rights](/wiki/Special_drawing_rights "Special drawing rights"), equivalent to an exchange rate of 1 US$ ≡ 0.709 dinar, or approximately 1 dinar ≡ 1.41044 dollars.[[186]](#cite_note-189) In 2000, Jordan joined the [World Trade Organisation](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") and signed the [Jordan–United States Free Trade Agreement](/wiki/Jordan%E2%80%93United_States_Free_Trade_Agreement "Jordan–United States Free Trade Agreement"), thus becoming the first Arab country to establish a free trade agreement with the United States. Jordan enjoys advanced status with the EU, which has facilitated greater access to export to European markets.[[187]](#cite_note-190) Due to slow domestic growth, high energy and food subsidies and a bloated [public-sector](/wiki/Public_sector "Public sector") workforce, Jordan usually runs annual [budget deficits](/wiki/Budget_deficit "Budget deficit").[[188]](#cite_note-congresser-191)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Jamal_Abdul_Nasser_Circle_Amman_Jordan.jpg/250px-Jamal_Abdul_Nasser_Circle_Amman_Jordan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jamal_Abdul_Nasser_Circle_Amman_Jordan.jpg)

View of a part of the capital [Amman](/wiki/Amman "Amman")

The [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession") and the turmoil caused by the [Arab Spring](/wiki/Arab_Spring "Arab Spring") have depressed GDP growth, damaging trade, industry, construction and tourism.[[14]](#cite_note-CIA_factbook-17) the number of tourist arrivals dropped sharply after 2011.[[189]](#cite_note-ftharsh-192) Between 2011 and 2016, the [natural gas pipeline](/wiki/Arab_Gas_Pipeline "Arab Gas Pipeline") in [Sinai](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula") supplying Jordan from Egypt was attacked 32 times by Islamic State affiliates. Jordan incurred billions of dollars in losses because it had to substitute more expensive heavy-fuel oils to generate electricity.[[190]](#cite_note-193) In 2012, the government cut subsidies on fuel, increasing its price.[[191]](#cite_note-oxbiz-194) The decision, which was later revoked, caused large scale protests to break out across the country.[[188]](#cite_note-congresser-191)[[189]](#cite_note-ftharsh-192)

Jordan's foreign debt in 2011 was $19 billion, representing 60% of its GDP. In 2016, the debt reached $35.1 billion representing 93% of its GDP.[[112]](#cite_note-instability1-115) This substantial increase is attributed to effects of regional instability causing a decrease in tourist activity, decreased foreign investments, increased military expenditures, attacks on Egyptian pipelines, the collapse of trade with Iraq and Syria, expenses from hosting Syrian refugees, and accumulated interest from loans.[[112]](#cite_note-instability1-115) According to the World Bank, Syrian refugees have cost Jordan more than $2.5 billion per year, amounting to 6% of the GDP and 25% of the government's annual revenue.[[192]](#cite_note-195) Foreign aid covers only a small part of these costs, 63% of the total costs are covered by Jordan.[[193]](#cite_note-196) An austerity programme was adopted by the government which aims to reduce the [debt-to-GDP ratio](/wiki/Debt-to-GDP_ratio "Debt-to-GDP ratio") to 77 percent by 2021.[[194]](#cite_note-197) The programme succeeded in preventing the debt from rising above 95% in 2018.[[195]](#cite_note-198)

The proportion of well-educated and skilled workers is among the highest in the region in sectors such as ICT and industry, due to a relatively modern educational system. This has attracted large foreign investments and has enabled the country to export its workforce to [Persian Gulf countries](/wiki/Arab_states_of_the_Persian_Gulf "Arab states of the Persian Gulf").[[11]](#cite_note-skwf-14) Flows of [remittances](/wiki/Remittance "Remittance") grew rapidly, particularly during the end of the 1970s and 1980s, and remains an important source of external funding.[[196]](#cite_note-199) Remittances were $3.8 billion in 2015, making Jordan the fourth-largest recipient in the region.[[197]](#cite_note-200)

### Transportation

Main article: [Transport in Jordan](/wiki/Transport_in_Jordan "Transport in Jordan")

Jordan is ranked as having the 35th best infrastructure in the world, one of the highest rankings in the developing world, according to the 2010 World Economic Forum's Index of Economic Competitiveness. This high infrastructural development is necessitated by its role as a transit country for goods and services mainly to Palestine and Iraq.[[198]](#cite_note-www3.weforum.org-201)

According to data from the Ministry of Public Works and Housing, as of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Jordan&action=edit), the road network consisted of 2,878 km (1,788 mi) of main roads; 2,592 km (1,611 mi) of rural roads and 1,733 km (1,077 mi) of side roads. The [Hejaz railway](/wiki/Hejaz_railway "Hejaz railway"), built during the Ottoman Empire which extended from Damascus to Mecca, will act as a base for future railway expansion plans. Currently, the railway has little civilian activity; it is primarily used for transporting goods. A national railway project is currently undergoing studies and seeking funding sources.[[199]](#cite_note-202) Amman has a network of public transportation buses including the [Amman Bus](/wiki/Amman_Bus "Amman Bus") and the [Amman Bus Rapid Transit](/wiki/Amman_Bus_Rapid_Transit "Amman Bus Rapid Transit") and is connected to nearby [Zarqa](/wiki/Zarqa "Zarqa") through the [Amman-Zarqa Bus Rapid Transit](/wiki/Amman-Zarqa_Bus_Rapid_Transit "Amman-Zarqa Bus Rapid Transit").

Jordan has three commercial airports, all receiving and dispatching international flights. Two are in Amman and the third is in Aqaba, [King Hussein International Airport](/wiki/King_Hussein_International_Airport "King Hussein International Airport"). [Amman Civil Airport](/wiki/Amman_Civil_Airport "Amman Civil Airport") serves several regional routes and charter flights while [Queen Alia International Airport](/wiki/Queen_Alia_International_Airport "Queen Alia International Airport") is the major international airport in Jordan and is the [hub](/wiki/Airline_hub "Airline hub") for [Royal Jordanian Airlines](/wiki/Royal_Jordanian_Airlines "Royal Jordanian Airlines"), the [flag carrier](/wiki/Flag_carrier "Flag carrier"). Queen Alia International Airport expansion was completed in 2013 with new terminals costing $700 million, to handle over 16 million passengers annually.[[200]](#cite_note-203) It is considered a state-of-the-art airport and was awarded 'the best airport by region: Middle East' for 2014 and 2015 by [Airport Service Quality](/wiki/List_of_Airport_Service_Quality_Award_winners "List of Airport Service Quality Award winners") survey, an airport passenger satisfaction benchmark programme.[[201]](#cite_note-204)

The [Port of Aqaba](/wiki/Port_of_Aqaba "Port of Aqaba") is the only port in Jordan. In 2006, the port was ranked as being the "Best Container Terminal" in the Middle East by *[Lloyd's List](/wiki/Lloyd%27s_List "Lloyd's List")*. The port was chosen because it is a port for other neighbouring countries, its location is between four countries and three continents, it is an exclusive gateway for the local market, and it has been recently improved.[[202]](#cite_note-205)

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in Jordan](/wiki/Tourism_in_Jordan "Tourism in Jordan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Bethany_%285%29.JPG/250px-Bethany_%285%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bethany_(5).JPG)

[Al-Maghtas](/wiki/Al-Maghtas "Al-Maghtas") ruins on the Jordanian side of the [Jordan River](/wiki/Jordan_River "Jordan River"), believed to have been the location of the [Baptism of Jesus](/wiki/Baptism_of_Jesus "Baptism of Jesus") and the ministry of [John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist")

The tourism sector is considered a cornerstone of the economy and is a large source of employment, hard currency, and economic growth. In 2010, there were 8 million visitors to Jordan. The majority of tourists are from European and Arab countries.[[12]](#cite_note-tourists-15) Tourism has been severely affected by regional turbulence,[[203]](#cite_note-Jordan_in_transition-206) notably those caused by the Arab Spring. Jordan experienced a 70% decrease in the number of tourists from 2010 to 2016;[[204]](#cite_note-Tourism_AJ-207) tourist numbers started to recover in 2017.[[204]](#cite_note-Tourism_AJ-207)

According to the [Ministry of Tourism and Antiquities](/wiki/Ministry_of_Tourism_and_Antiquities_(Jordan) "Ministry of Tourism and Antiquities (Jordan)"), Jordan is home to around 100,000 archaeological and tourist sites.[[205]](#cite_note-208) Some very well preserved historical cities include [Petra](/wiki/Petra "Petra") and [Jerash](/wiki/Jerash "Jerash"), the former being the most popular tourist attraction and an icon of the kingdom.[[204]](#cite_note-Tourism_AJ-207) As part of the [Holy Land](/wiki/Holy_Land "Holy Land"), there are numerous biblical sites, including: [Al-Maghtas](/wiki/Al-Maghtas "Al-Maghtas") (a traditional location for the [Baptism of Jesus](/wiki/Baptism_of_Jesus "Baptism of Jesus")), [Mount Nebo](/wiki/Mount_Nebo "Mount Nebo"), [Umm ar-Rasas](/wiki/Umm_ar-Rasas "Umm ar-Rasas"), [Madaba](/wiki/Madaba "Madaba") and [Machaerus](/wiki/Machaerus "Machaerus").[[206]](#cite_note-209) Islamic sites include shrines of the prophet [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad")'s companions such as [Abd Allah ibn Rawahah](/wiki/Abd_Allah_ibn_Rawahah "Abd Allah ibn Rawahah"), [Zayd ibn Harithah](/wiki/Zayd_ibn_Haritha_al-Kalbi "Zayd ibn Haritha al-Kalbi") and [Muadh ibn Jabal](/wiki/Muadh_ibn_Jabal "Muadh ibn Jabal").[[207]](#cite_note-210) [Ajloun Castle](/wiki/Ajloun_Castle "Ajloun Castle"), built by Muslim Ayyubid leader [Saladin](/wiki/Saladin "Saladin") in the 12th century during his wars with the Crusaders, is also a popular tourist attraction.[[118]](#cite_note-mattel-121)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Dana_Reserve_07.jpg/250px-Dana_Reserve_07.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dana_Reserve_07.jpg)

The [Dana Biosphere Reserve](/wiki/Dana_Biosphere_Reserve "Dana Biosphere Reserve") in southern Jordan lies along the [Jordan Trail](/wiki/Jordan_Trail "Jordan Trail"), a hiking path that is gaining popularity.

Modern entertainment, recreation and souqs in urban areas, mostly in Amman, also attract tourists. Recently, the nightlife in Amman, Aqaba and Irbid has started to emerge and the number of bars, discos and nightclubs is on the rise.[[208]](#cite_note-211) Alcohol is widely available in tourist restaurants, liquor stores and even some supermarkets.[[209]](#cite_note-lpgw-212) Valleys including [Wadi Mujib](/wiki/Wadi_Mujib "Wadi Mujib") and hiking trails in different parts of the country attract adventurers. Hiking is gaining popularity among tourists and locals. Places such as Dana Biosphere Reserve and Petra offer numerous signposted hiking trails. The [Jordan Trail](/wiki/Jordan_Trail "Jordan Trail"), a 650 km (400 mi) hiking trail stretching the entire country from north to south, crossing several attractions, was established in 2015.[[210]](#cite_note-JT1-213) The trail aims to revive the tourism sector.[[210]](#cite_note-JT1-213) Moreover, seaside recreation is present on the shores of Aqaba and the Dead Sea through several international resorts.[[211]](#cite_note-214)

Jordan has been a [medical tourism](/wiki/Medical_tourism "Medical tourism") destination in the Middle East since the 1970s. A study conducted by [Jordan's Private Hospitals Association](/wiki/Private_Hospitals_Association_(Jordan) "Private Hospitals Association (Jordan)") found that 250,000 patients from 102 countries received treatment in Jordan in 2010, compared to 190,000 in 2007, bringing over $1 billion in revenue. Jordan is the region's top medical tourism destination, as rated by the World Bank, and fifth in the world overall.[[212]](#cite_note-Ha'aretz_medical-215) The majority of patients come from Yemen, Libya and Syria because of the ongoing civil wars in those countries. Doctors and medical staff have gained experience in dealing with war patients through years of receiving such cases from various conflict zones in the region.[[213]](#cite_note-216)

Natural treatment methods can be found in both [Ma'in Hot Springs](/wiki/Ma%27in_Hot_Springs "Ma'in Hot Springs") and the Dead Sea. The Dead Sea is often described as a 'natural spa'. It contains 10 times more salt than the average ocean, which makes it impossible to sink in. The high salinity of the Dead Sea has been proven therapeutic for many skin diseases.[[214]](#cite_note-217) The uniqueness of this lake attracts several Jordanian and foreign vacationers, which boosted investments in the hotel sector in the area.[[215]](#cite_note-218)

### Natural resources

See also: [Energy in Jordan](/wiki/Energy_in_Jordan "Energy in Jordan")

Jordan is among the most water-scarce nations on Earth. At 97 cubic metres of water per person per year, it is considered to face "absolute [water scarcity](/wiki/Water_scarcity "Water scarcity")" according to the Falkenmark Classification.[[216]](#cite_note-:1-219) Scarce resources to begin with have been aggravated by the massive influx of Syrian refugees, many of whom face issues of access to clean water in informal settlements (see "Immigrants and Refugees" below).[[217]](#cite_note-220) Jordan shares both of its two main surface water resources, the Jordan and Yarmuk rivers, with neighbouring countries, adding complexity to water allocation decisions.[[216]](#cite_note-:1-219) Water from [Disi aquifer](/wiki/Disi_Water_Conveyance "Disi Water Conveyance") and ten major dams historically played a large role in providing fresh water.[[218]](#cite_note-jordan-water-221) The [Jawa Dam](/wiki/Jawa_Dam_(Jordan) "Jawa Dam (Jordan)") in northeastern Jordan, which dates back to the fourth millennium BC, is the world's oldest dam.[[219]](#cite_note-222)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Aqaba_Railway_Corporation_BW_1.JPG/250px-Aqaba_Railway_Corporation_BW_1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Aqaba_Railway_Corporation_BW_1.JPG)

A phosphate train at Ram station

Natural gas was discovered in 1987; however, the estimated size of the reserve discovered was about 230 billion [cubic feet](/wiki/Cubic_foot "Cubic foot"), a minuscule quantity compared with its oil-rich neighbours. The Risha field, in the eastern desert beside the Iraqi border, produces nearly 35 million cubic feet of gas per day, which is sent to a nearby power plant to generate a small amount of Jordan's electricity needs.[[220]](#cite_note-223) This led to a reliance on importing oil to generate almost all of its electricity. Regional instability over the decades halted oil and gas supply to the kingdom from various sources, making it incur billions of dollars in losses. Jordan built a [liquified natural gas](/wiki/Liquified_natural_gas "Liquified natural gas") port in Aqaba in 2012 to temporarily substitute the supply, while formulating a strategy to rationalise energy consumption and to diversify its energy sources.

Jordan receives 330 days of sunshine per year, and wind speeds reach over 7 m/s in the mountainous areas, so renewables proved a promising sector.[[221]](#cite_note-224) King Abdullah inaugurated large-scale renewable energy projects in the 2010s including the 117 MW [Tafila Wind Farm](/wiki/Tafila_Wind_Farm "Tafila Wind Farm"), the 53 MW [Shams Ma'an](/wiki/Shams_Ma%27an_Solar_Power_Plant "Shams Ma'an Solar Power Plant"), and the 103 MW [Quweira](/wiki/Quweira_Solar_Power_Plant "Quweira Solar Power Plant") solar power plants, with several more projects planned. By early 2019, it was reported that more than 1090 MW of renewable energy projects had been completed, contributing to 8% of Jordan's electricity up from 3% in 2011, while 92% was generated from gas.[[222]](#cite_note-225) After having initially set the percentage of renewable energy, Jordan aimed to generate by 2020 at 10%, the government announced in 2018 that it sought to beat that figure and aim for 20%.[[223]](#cite_note-226)

Jordan has the fifth largest [oil-shale](/wiki/Oil_shale "Oil shale") reserves in the world, which could be commercially exploited in the central and northwestern regions.[[224]](#cite_note-economist.com-227) Official figures estimate reserves at more than 70 billion tonnes. [Attarat Power Plant](/wiki/Attarat_Power_Plant "Attarat Power Plant"), its first oil-shale power plant, was commissioned in 2023, with a 470 MW capacity.[[225]](#cite_note-228) Jordan also aims to benefit from its large uranium reserves by tapping nuclear energy. The original plan involved constructing two 1,000 MW reactors but has been scrapped because of financial constraints.[[226]](#cite_note-229) Currently, the [Atomic Energy Commission](/wiki/Jordan_Atomic_Energy_Commission "Jordan Atomic Energy Commission") is considering building [small modular reactors](/wiki/Small_modular_reactor "Small modular reactor") instead, whose capacities hover below 500 MW and can provide water sources through [desalination](/wiki/Desalination "Desalination"). In 2018, the commission announced that Jordan was in talks with multiple companies to build its first commercial nuclear plant, a helium-cooled reactor that is scheduled for completion by 2025.[[227]](#cite_note-230) [Phosphate](/wiki/Phosphorite "Phosphorite") mines in the south have made Jordan one of the largest producers and exporters of the mineral in the world.[[228]](#cite_note-231)

### Industry

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Aqaba.jpg/250px-Aqaba.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aqaba.jpg)

The [Aqaba Flagpole](/wiki/Aqaba_Flagpole "Aqaba Flagpole") in the southernmost city of [Aqaba](/wiki/Aqaba "Aqaba"), Jordan's only coastal outlet

The industrial sector, which includes mining, manufacturing, construction, and power, accounted for approximately 26% of the GDP in 2004 (including manufacturing, 16.2%; construction, 4.6%; and mining, 3.1%). More than 21% of the labor force was employed in industry in 2002. In 2014, industry accounted for 6% of the GDP.[[229]](#cite_note-232) The main industrial products are potash, phosphates, cement, clothes, and fertilisers. The most promising segment of this sector is construction. Petra Engineering Industries Company, which is considered to be one of the main pillars of Jordanian industry, has gained international recognition with its air-conditioning units reaching [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA").[[230]](#cite_note-233) Jordan is a pharmaceuticals manufacturer in the [MENA](/wiki/Middle_East_and_North_Africa "Middle East and North Africa") region, led by [Hikma](/wiki/Hikma_Pharmaceuticals "Hikma Pharmaceuticals").[[231]](#cite_note-234)

The military industry thrived after the [Jordan Design and Development Bureau](/wiki/Jordan_Design_and_Development_Bureau "Jordan Design and Development Bureau") defence company was established by King Abdullah II in 1999, to provide an indigenous capability for the supply of scientific and technical services to the Jordanian Armed Forces, and to become a global hub in security research and development. It manufactures all types of military products, many of which are presented at the bi-annually held international military exhibition [SOFEX](/wiki/Special_Operations_Forces_Exhibition "Special Operations Forces Exhibition"). In 2015, the company exported $72 million worth of industries to over 42 countries.[[232]](#cite_note-235)

### Science and technology

Main article: [Science and technology in Jordan](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Jordan "Science and technology in Jordan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Tafila_Wind_Farm_2.jpg/250px-Tafila_Wind_Farm_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tafila_Wind_Farm_2.jpg)

The 117 MW [Tafila Wind Farm](/wiki/Tafila_Wind_Farm "Tafila Wind Farm") in southern Jordan is the first and largest commercial wind farm in the Middle East.[[233]](#cite_note-236)

Science and technology is the fastest developing economic sector. This growth is occurring across multiple industries, including information and communications technology (ICT) and nuclear technology. Jordan contributes 75% of the Arabic content on the Internet.[[234]](#cite_note-usdpict-237) In 2014, the ICT sector accounted for more than 84,000 jobs and contributed to 12% of the GDP. More than 400 companies are active in telecom, information technology, and video game development. 600 companies are operating in active technologies and 300 start-up companies.[[234]](#cite_note-usdpict-237) Jordan was ranked 65th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[235]](#cite_note-238)[[236]](#cite_note-239)
Nuclear science and technology are also expanding. The [Jordan Research and Training Reactor](/wiki/Jordan_Research_and_Training_Reactor "Jordan Research and Training Reactor"), which was commissioned in 2016, is a 5 MW training reactor located at the [Jordan University of Science and Technology](/wiki/Jordan_University_of_Science_and_Technology "Jordan University of Science and Technology") in [Ar Ramtha](/wiki/Ar_Ramtha "Ar Ramtha").[[237]](#cite_note-jrtrjt-240) The facility is the first nuclear reactor in the country and will provide Jordan with radioactive isotopes for medical usage and provide training to students to produce a skilled workforce for the country's planned commercial nuclear reactors.[[237]](#cite_note-jrtrjt-240)

Jordan also hosts the [Synchrotron-Light for Experimental Science and Applications in the Middle East](/wiki/Synchrotron-Light_for_Experimental_Science_and_Applications_in_the_Middle_East "Synchrotron-Light for Experimental Science and Applications in the Middle East") (SESAME) facility, which is the only particle accelerator in the Middle East, and one of only 60 [synchrotron](/wiki/Synchrotron "Synchrotron") radiation facilities in the world.[[238]](#cite_note-nytsrf-241) SESAME, supported by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") and [CERN](/wiki/CERN "CERN"), was opened in 2017 and allows for collaboration between scientists from various rival Middle Eastern countries.[[238]](#cite_note-nytsrf-241)

Main article: [Demographics of Jordan](/wiki/Demographics_of_Jordan "Demographics of Jordan")

See also: [List of cities in Jordan](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Jordan "List of cities in Jordan")

The 2015 census showed a population of 9,531,712 (female: 47%; males: 53%). Around 2.9 million (30%) were non-citizens, a figure including refugees and illegal immigrants.[[4]](#cite_note-census-4) There were 1,977,534 households in 2015, with an average of 4.8 persons per household, compared to 6.7 persons per household for the census of 1979.[[4]](#cite_note-census-4) The population of Amman was 65,754 in 1946, but exceeded 4 million in 2015.

[Arabs](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs") make up about 98% of the population. The remaining 2% consist largely of peoples from the Caucasus including [Circassians](/wiki/Circassians "Circassians"), [Armenians](/wiki/Armenians "Armenians"), and [Chechens](/wiki/Chechens "Chechens"), along with smaller minority groups.[[14]](#cite_note-CIA_factbook-17) About 84.1% of the population live in urban areas.[[14]](#cite_note-CIA_factbook-17)

### Refugees, immigrants and expatriates

Jordan was home to 2,175,491 Palestinian refugees in December 2016. Most of them had been granted Jordanian citizenship.[[240]](#cite_note-wwwj-243) The first wave of Palestinian refugees arrived during the 1948 Arab–Israeli War and peaked in the 1967 Six-Day War and the 1990 Gulf War. In the past, Jordan had given many Palestinian refugees citizenship, however recently citizenship is given only in rare cases. 370,000 of these Palestinians live in UNRWA refugee camps.[[240]](#cite_note-wwwj-243) Following the capture of the West Bank by Israel in 1967, Jordan revoked the citizenship of thousands of Palestinians to thwart any attempt to permanently resettle from the West Bank to Jordan. West Bank Palestinians with family in Jordan or Jordanian citizenship were issued yellow cards guaranteeing them all the rights of citizenship if requested.[[241]](#cite_note-revokecitizen-244)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/An_Aerial_View_of_the_Za%27atri_Refugee_Camp.jpg/250px-An_Aerial_View_of_the_Za%27atri_Refugee_Camp.jpg)](/wiki/File:An_Aerial_View_of_the_Za%27atri_Refugee_Camp.jpg)

An aerial view of a portion of the [Zaatari refugee camp](/wiki/Zaatari_refugee_camp "Zaatari refugee camp") which contains a population of 80,000 [Syrian refugees](/wiki/Syrians_in_Jordan "Syrians in Jordan"), the largest Syrian refugee camp in the world

Up to 1,000,000 [Iraqis moved to Jordan](/wiki/Iraqis_in_Jordan "Iraqis in Jordan") following the Iraq War in 2003,[[242]](#cite_note-245) and most of them returned. By 2015, their number was 130,911. Many Iraqi Christians, the vast majority of whom being ethnic [Assyrians](/wiki/Assyrian_people "Assyrian people"), settled temporarily or permanently in Jordan.[[243]](#cite_note-246) Immigrants include 15,000 Lebanese who arrived following the [2006 Lebanon War](/wiki/2006_Lebanon_War "2006 Lebanon War").[[244]](#cite_note-247) Since 2010, over 1.4 million [Syrian refugees](/wiki/Refugees_of_the_Syrian_civil_war "Refugees of the Syrian civil war") have fled to Jordan to escape the violence in Syria,[[4]](#cite_note-census-4) the largest population being in the [Zaatari refugee camp](/wiki/Zaatari_refugee_camp "Zaatari refugee camp").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The kingdom has continued to demonstrate hospitality, despite the substantial strain the flux of Syrian refugees places on Jordanian communities, as the vast majority of Syrian refugees do not live in camps. The refugee crisis effects include competition for job opportunities, water resources and other state provided services, along with the strain on the national infrastructure.[[10]](#cite_note-UNHCR-13)

In 2007, there were up to 150,000 [Assyrian](/wiki/Assyrian_People "Assyrian People") [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians"). Most are [Eastern Aramaic](/wiki/Eastern_Aramaic "Eastern Aramaic") speaking refugees from Iraq.[[245]](#cite_note-248) [Kurds](/wiki/Kurds "Kurds") number some 30,000, and like the Assyrians, many are refugees from Iraq, Iran and Turkey.[[246]](#cite_note-kurd-language-249) Descendants of [Armenians](/wiki/Armenians "Armenians") that sought refuge in the Levant during the 1915 [Armenian genocide](/wiki/Armenian_genocide "Armenian genocide") number approximately 5,000 persons, mainly residing in Amman.[[247]](#cite_note-250) A small number of ethnic [Mandeans](/wiki/Mandaeans "Mandaeans") live in Jordan, again mainly refugees from Iraq.[[248]](#cite_note-251)

Around 12,000 [Iraqi Christians](/wiki/Christianity_in_Iraq "Christianity in Iraq") have sought refuge in Jordan after the Islamic State took the city of [Mosul](/wiki/Mosul "Mosul") in 2014.[[249]](#cite_note-252) Several thousand Libyans, Yemenis and Sudanese have sought asylum to escape instability and violence in their respective countries.[[10]](#cite_note-UNHCR-13) The 2015 census recorded 1,265,000 Syrians, 636,270 Egyptians, 634,182 Palestinians, 130,911 Iraqis, 31,163 Yemenis, 22,700 Libyans and 197,385 from other nationalities residing in Jordan.[[4]](#cite_note-census-4)

In 2012, there were around 1.2 million illegal and 500,000 legal migrant workers and expatriates in the kingdom.[[250]](#cite_note-253) Thousands of foreign women, mostly from the Middle East and Eastern Europe, work in nightclubs, hotels and bars across the kingdom.[[251]](#cite_note-N._Zaqqa_2006,_p._11-254)[[252]](#cite_note-womendialogue1-255)[[253]](#cite_note-256) American and European expatriate communities are concentrated in the capital, as the city is home to many international organisations and diplomatic missions.[[209]](#cite_note-lpgw-212)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Jordan](/wiki/Religion_in_Jordan "Religion in Jordan")

[Sunni Islam](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam") is the dominant religion. Muslims make up about 95% of the population. 93% of those self-identify as Sunnis.[[254]](#cite_note-pewforum-257) There are a small number of [Ahmadi](/wiki/Ahmadiyya "Ahmadiyya") Muslims[[255]](#cite_note-258) and some [Shiites](/wiki/Shiite "Shiite"). Many Shia are Iraqi and Lebanese refugees.[[256]](#cite_note-259) Muslims who convert to another religion as well as missionaries from other religions face societal and legal discrimination.[[257]](#cite_note-260)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Aqaba_Mosque.jpg/250px-Aqaba_Mosque.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aqaba_Mosque.jpg)

[Marsa Zayed](/wiki/Marsa_Zayed "Marsa Zayed") mosque in [Aqaba](/wiki/Aqaba "Aqaba")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Orthodox_church_in_Amman1.jpg/250px-Orthodox_church_in_Amman1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Orthodox_church_in_Amman1.jpg)

[An eastern Orthodox church](/wiki/Christianity_in_Jordan "Christianity in Jordan") during a snowstorm in [Amman](/wiki/Amman "Amman")

Jordan contains some of the [oldest Christian communities](/wiki/Christianity_in_Jordan "Christianity in Jordan") in the world, dating as early as the 1st century AD after the [crucifixion of Jesus](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus").[[258]](#cite_note-261) Christians today make up about 4% of the population,[[259]](#cite_note-abouna-262) down from 20% in 1930, though their absolute number has grown.[[260]](#cite_note-thenational.ae-263) This is due to high immigration rates of Muslims into Jordan, higher emigration rates of Christians to the [West](/wiki/Western_world "Western world"), and higher birth rates for Muslims.[[261]](#cite_note-lat-264)

Christians number around 250,000, all of whom are Arabic-speaking, according to a 2014 estimate by the Orthodox Church, though the study excluded minority Christian groups and the thousands of Western, Iraqi and Syrian Christians residing in Jordan.[[259]](#cite_note-abouna-262) Christians are well integrated in society and enjoy a high level of freedom.[[262]](#cite_note-angilican-265) Christians are also influential in the media.[[263]](#cite_note-266)

Smaller religious minorities include [Druze](/wiki/Druze "Druze"), [Baháʼís](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith") and [Mandaeans](/wiki/Mandaeans "Mandaeans"). Most Druze live in Azraq, some villages on the Syrian border, and in Zarqa. Most Jordanian Baháʼís live in Adassiyeh bordering the Jordan Valley.[[264]](#cite_note-267) It is estimated that 1,400 Mandaeans live in Amman. They came from Iraq after the 2003 invasion fleeing persecution.[[265]](#cite_note-268)

### Languages

The official language is [Modern Standard Arabic](/wiki/Modern_Standard_Arabic "Modern Standard Arabic"), a literary language taught in the schools.[[266]](#cite_note-lang-269) Most Jordanians natively speak one of the non-standard Arabic dialects known as [Jordanian Arabic](/wiki/Jordanian_Arabic "Jordanian Arabic"). [Jordanian Sign Language](/wiki/Levantine_Arabic_Sign_Language "Levantine Arabic Sign Language") is the language of the deaf community. English, though without official status, is widely spoken throughout Jordan and is the *de facto* language of commerce and banking, as well as a co-official status in the education sector. Almost all university-level classes are held in English, and almost all public schools teach English along with Standard Arabic.[[266]](#cite_note-lang-269)

[Chechen](/wiki/Chechen_language "Chechen language"), [Circassian](/wiki/Circassian_languages "Circassian languages"), [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_language "Armenian language"), [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language"), and [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") are popular among their communities.[[267]](#cite_note-John_A._Shoup_2007-270) [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") is offered as an elective in many schools, mainly in the private sector.[[266]](#cite_note-lang-269) [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") is an increasingly popular language. It has been introduced at a larger scale since the establishment of the [German Jordanian University](/wiki/German_Jordanian_University "German Jordanian University") in 2005.[[268]](#cite_note-271)

### Health and education

Main articles: [Health in Jordan](/wiki/Health_in_Jordan "Health in Jordan") and [Education in Jordan](/wiki/Education_in_Jordan "Education in Jordan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Young_girls_reading_-_Government_primary_school_in_Amman%2C_Jordan.jpg/250px-Young_girls_reading_-_Government_primary_school_in_Amman%2C_Jordan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Young_girls_reading_-_Government_primary_school_in_Amman,_Jordan.jpg)

Jordanian school girls pictured reading in a public school. Jordan's youth female literacy rate (15 – 24 years) was 99.37% in 2015.[[269]](#cite_note-:0-272)

Life expectancy in Jordan was around 74.8 years in 2017.[[14]](#cite_note-CIA_factbook-17) The leading cause of death is cardiovascular diseases, followed by cancer.[[270]](#cite_note-273) Childhood immunisation rates have increased steadily over the past 15 years. In 2002, immunisations and [vaccines](/wiki/Vaccine "Vaccine") reached more than 95% of children under five.[[271]](#cite_note-cp-274) In 1950, [water and sanitation](/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_Jordan "Water supply and sanitation in Jordan") was available to 10% of the population. In 2015, it reached 98% of Jordanians.[[272]](#cite_note-275)

Health services are some of the best in the region.[[273]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-276) Qualified medics, a favourable investment climate, and economic stability have contributed to the success of this sector.[[274]](#cite_note-277) The health care system is divided between public and private institutions. In 2007, [Jordan Hospital](/wiki/Jordan_Hospital "Jordan Hospital"), as the biggest private hospital, was the first general specialty hospital to gain the international accreditation [JCAHO](/wiki/Joint_Commission "Joint Commission").[[271]](#cite_note-cp-274) The [King Hussein Cancer Centre](/wiki/King_Hussein_Cancer_Center "King Hussein Cancer Center") is a cancer treatment centre.[[275]](#cite_note-278) 66% of Jordanians have medical insurance.[[4]](#cite_note-census-4)

The educational system comprises 2 years of pre-school education, 10 years of compulsory basic education, and two years of secondary academic or vocational education, after which the students sit for the General Certificate of Secondary Education Exam (*[Tawjihi](/wiki/Tawjihi "Tawjihi"))* exams.[[276]](#cite_note-279) Primary education is free.[[277]](#cite_note-280) Scholars may attend either private or public schools. According to the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO"), the literacy rate in 2015 was 98.01% and is considered to be the highest in the Middle East and the Arab world, and one of the highest in the world.[[269]](#cite_note-:0-272)

In 2009, UNESCO ranked Jordan's educational system 18th out of 94 nations for providing gender equality in education.[[278]](#cite_note-281) Jordan has the highest number of researchers in research and development per million people, among all the 57 countries that are members of the [Organisation of Islamic Cooperation](/wiki/Organisation_of_Islamic_Cooperation "Organisation of Islamic Cooperation"). There are 8,060 researchers per million people, while the world average is 2,532 per million.[[279]](#cite_note-282)

Jordan has 10 public universities, 19 private universities and 54 community colleges, of which 14 are public, 24 private and others affiliated with the Jordanian Armed Forces, the Civil Defence Department, the Ministry of Health and UNRWA.[[280]](#cite_note-283) In 2016, there were over 200,000 students enrolled in universities each year. An additional 20,000 pursue higher education abroad primarily in the United States and Europe.[[281]](#cite_note-284) According to the [Webometrics Ranking of World Universities](/wiki/Webometrics_Ranking_of_World_Universities "Webometrics Ranking of World Universities"), the top-ranking universities in Jordan are the [University of Jordan](/wiki/University_of_Jordan "University of Jordan") (UJ) (1,220th worldwide), [Jordan University of Science & Technology](/wiki/Jordan_University_of_Science_%26_Technology "Jordan University of Science & Technology") (JUST) (1,729th) and [Hashemite University](/wiki/Hashemite_University "Hashemite University") (2,176th).[[282]](#cite_note-285) UJ and JUST occupy 8th and 10th between Arab universities.[[283]](#cite_note-286)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Jordan](/wiki/Culture_of_Jordan "Culture of Jordan")

### Art and museums

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Pipers_jordaniens_%28Jerash%29_%286949582694%29.jpg/250px-Pipers_jordaniens_%28Jerash%29_%286949582694%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pipers_jordaniens_(Jerash)_(6949582694).jpg)

Jordanian folklore band playing [bagpipes](/wiki/Bagpipes "Bagpipes") in [Jerash](/wiki/Jerash "Jerash")

Many institutions aim to increase cultural awareness of [Jordanian art](/wiki/Jordanian_art "Jordanian art") and to represent artistic movements in fields such as paintings, sculpture, graffiti and photography.[[284]](#cite_note-287) The art scene has been developing in the past few years,[[285]](#cite_note-288) and Jordan has been a haven for artists from surrounding countries.[[286]](#cite_note-289) In January 2016, for the first time ever, a [Jordanian film](/wiki/Cinema_of_Jordan "Cinema of Jordan") called [Theeb](/wiki/Theeb "Theeb") was nominated for the [Academy Awards for Best Foreign Language Film](/wiki/List_of_submissions_to_the_88th_Academy_Awards_for_Best_Foreign_Language_Film "List of submissions to the 88th Academy Awards for Best Foreign Language Film").[[287]](#cite_note-290)

The largest museum is [The Jordan Museum](/wiki/The_Jordan_Museum "The Jordan Museum"). It contains much of the valuable archaeological findings in the country, including some of the [Dead Sea Scrolls](/wiki/Dead_Sea_Scrolls "Dead Sea Scrolls"), the Neolithic limestone statues of ['Ain Ghazal](/wiki/Ayn_Ghazal_(archaeological_site) "Ayn Ghazal (archaeological site)") and a copy of the [Mesha Stele](/wiki/Mesha_Stele "Mesha Stele").[[288]](#cite_note-291) Most museums are located in Amman including [The Children's Museum Jordan](/wiki/The_Children%27s_Museum_Jordan "The Children's Museum Jordan"), [The Martyr's Memorial and Museum](/wiki/The_Martyr%27s_Memorial_and_Museum "The Martyr's Memorial and Museum") and the [Royal Automobile Museum](/wiki/Royal_Automobile_Museum "Royal Automobile Museum"). Museums outside Amman include the [Aqaba Archaeological Museum](/wiki/Aqaba_Archaeological_Museum "Aqaba Archaeological Museum").[[289]](#cite_note-Bradt-292) The [Jordan National Gallery of Fine Arts](/wiki/Jordan_National_Gallery_of_Fine_Arts "Jordan National Gallery of Fine Arts") is a major contemporary art museum located in Amman.[[289]](#cite_note-Bradt-292)

[Music in Jordan](/wiki/Music_of_Jordan "Music of Jordan") is developing a lot of new bands and artists who are popular in the Middle East. Artists such as [Omar Al-Abdallat](/wiki/Omar_Al-Abdallat "Omar Al-Abdallat"), [Toni Qattan](/wiki/Toni_Qattan "Toni Qattan"), [Diana Karazon](/wiki/Diana_Karazon "Diana Karazon") and [Hani Mitwasi](/wiki/Hani_Mitwasi "Hani Mitwasi") have increased the popularity of Jordanian music.[[290]](#cite_note-jf-293) The [Jerash Festival](/wiki/Jerash_Festival "Jerash Festival") is an annual music event that features popular Arab singers.[[290]](#cite_note-jf-293) Pianist and composer [Zade Dirani](/wiki/Zade_Dirani "Zade Dirani") has gained wide international popularity.[[291]](#cite_note-294) There is also an increasing growth of alternative [Arabic rock](/wiki/Arabic_rock "Arabic rock") bands, who are dominating the scene in the Arab world, including: [El Morabba3](/wiki/El_Morabba3 "El Morabba3"), [Autostrad](/wiki/Autostrad "Autostrad"), [JadaL](/wiki/JadaL "JadaL"), [Akher Zapheer](/wiki/Akher_Zapheer "Akher Zapheer") and [Aziz Maraka](/wiki/Aziz_Maraka "Aziz Maraka").[[292]](#cite_note-295)

Jordan unveiled its first underwater military museum off the coast of Aqaba. Several military vehicles, including tanks, troop carriers and a helicopter are in the museum.[[293]](#cite_note-296)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Jordanian cuisine](/wiki/Jordanian_cuisine "Jordanian cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Pr%C3%A9paration_du_mansaf-Jordanie_%288%29.jpg/250px-Pr%C3%A9paration_du_mansaf-Jordanie_%288%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pr%C3%A9paration_du_mansaf-Jordanie_(8).jpg)

[Mansaf](/wiki/Mansaf "Mansaf"), the traditional dish of Jordan, originates from Bedouin life and is a symbol of Jordanian hospitality.

As the eighth-largest producer of [olives](/wiki/Olive "Olive") in the world, [olive oil](/wiki/Olive_oil "Olive oil") is the main cooking oil in Jordan.[[294]](#cite_note-297) A common appetiser is [hummus](/wiki/Hummus "Hummus"), which is a puree of [chickpeas](/wiki/Chickpea "Chickpea") blended with [tahini](/wiki/Tahini "Tahini"), lemon, and garlic. [Ful medames](/wiki/Ful_medames "Ful medames") is another well-known appetiser. A typical worker's meal, it has since made its way to the tables of the upper class. A typical [meze](/wiki/Meze "Meze") often contains [koubba maqliya](/wiki/Kibbeh "Kibbeh"), [labaneh](/wiki/Strained_yogurt "Strained yogurt"), [baba ghanoush](/wiki/Baba_ghanoush "Baba ghanoush"), [tabbouleh](/wiki/Tabbouleh "Tabbouleh"), [olives](/wiki/Olive "Olive") and [pickles](/wiki/Pickled_cucumber "Pickled cucumber").[[295]](#cite_note-Mansaf-298) Meze is generally accompanied by the Levantine alcoholic drink [arak](/wiki/Arak_(drink) "Arak (drink)"), which is made from grapes and aniseed and is similar to [ouzo](/wiki/Ouzo "Ouzo"), [rakı](/wiki/Rak%C4%B1 "Rakı") and [pastis](/wiki/Pastis "Pastis"). [Jordanian wine](/wiki/Jordanian_wine "Jordanian wine") and [beer](/wiki/Beer_in_Jordan "Beer in Jordan") are also sometimes used. The same dishes, served without alcoholic drinks, can also be termed "muqabbilat" (starters) in Arabic.[[209]](#cite_note-lpgw-212)

The most distinctive dish is [mansaf](/wiki/Mansaf "Mansaf"), the national dish of Jordan. The dish is a symbol for hospitality and is influenced by the Bedouin culture. Mansaf is eaten on different occasions such as funerals, weddings and on religious holidays. It consists of a plate of rice with meat that was boiled in thick yogurt, sprinkled with pine nuts and sometimes herbs. As an old tradition, the dish is eaten using one's hands, but the tradition is not always used.[[295]](#cite_note-Mansaf-298) Simple fresh fruit is often served towards the end of a meal, but there is also dessert, such as [baklava](/wiki/Baklava "Baklava"), hareeseh, [knafeh](/wiki/Knafeh "Knafeh"), [halva](/wiki/Halva "Halva") and [qatayef](/wiki/Qatayef "Qatayef"), a dish made specially for [Ramadan](/wiki/Ramadan "Ramadan"). Drinking coffee and tea flavoured with [na'na](/wiki/Mentha "Mentha") or [meramiyyeh](/wiki/Salvia_officinalis "Salvia officinalis") is commonplace.[[296]](#cite_note-299)

### Sports

Main article: [Sport in Jordan](/wiki/Sport_in_Jordan "Sport in Jordan")

While both team and individual sports are widely played, the kingdom has enjoyed its biggest international achievements in [taekwondo](/wiki/Taekwondo "Taekwondo"). The highlight came at the [2016 Rio Olympic Games](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics "2016 Summer Olympics") when [Ahmad Abughaush](/wiki/Ahmad_Abughaush "Ahmad Abughaush") won Jordan's first ever medal[[297]](#cite_note-300) of any colour at the games by taking gold in the −67 kg weight.[[298]](#cite_note-301) Medals have continued to be won at world and Asian level in the sport since to establish taekwondo as the kingdom's favourite sport alongside [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football")[[209]](#cite_note-lpgw-212) and [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball").[[299]](#cite_note-302)

Football is the most popular sport.[[300]](#cite_note-303) The [national football team](/wiki/Jordan_national_football_team "Jordan national football team") came within a play-off of reaching the [2014 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup "2014 FIFA World Cup") in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"),[[301]](#cite_note-304) but lost the [two-legged tie](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(inter-confederation_play-offs) "2014 FIFA World Cup qualification (inter-confederation play-offs)") against [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay_national_football_team "Uruguay national football team").[[302]](#cite_note-305) They previously reached the quarter-finals of the [AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/AFC_Asian_Cup "AFC Asian Cup") in [2004](/wiki/2004_AFC_Asian_Cup "2004 AFC Asian Cup") and [2011](/wiki/2011_AFC_Asian_Cup "2011 AFC Asian Cup"), and lost in the [final](/wiki/2023_AFC_Asian_Cup_final "2023 AFC Asian Cup final") against [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar_national_football_team "Qatar national football team") in [2023](/wiki/2023_AFC_Asian_Cup "2023 AFC Asian Cup").[[303]](#cite_note-306)

Jordan has a strong policy for inclusive sport and invests heavily in encouraging girls and women to participate in all sports. The [women's football team](/wiki/Jordan_women%27s_national_football_team "Jordan women's national football team") gaining reputation,[[304]](#cite_note-307) and in March 2016 ranked 58th in the world.[[305]](#cite_note-308) In 2016, Jordan hosted the [FIFA U-17 Women's World Cup](/wiki/2016_FIFA_U-17_Women%27s_World_Cup "2016 FIFA U-17 Women's World Cup"), with 16 teams representing six continents. The tournament was held in four stadiums in the three Jordanian cities of Amman, Zarqa and Irbid. It was the first women's sports tournament in the Middle East.[[306]](#cite_note-309)

Basketball is another sport that Jordan continues to excel in, having qualified to the [FIBA 2010 World Basketball Cup](/wiki/2010_FIBA_World_Championship "2010 FIBA World Championship") and more recently reaching the [2019 World Cup in China](/wiki/2019_FIBA_Basketball_World_Cup "2019 FIBA Basketball World Cup").[[307]](#cite_note-310) Jordan came within a point of reaching the [2012 Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics") after losing the final of the 2010 Asian Cup to China, 70–69, and settling for silver instead. The [national basketball team](/wiki/Jordan_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Jordan men's national basketball team") participates in various international and Middle Eastern tournaments. Local basketball teams include: Al-Orthodoxi Club, Al-Riyadi, Zain, Al-Hussein and Al-Jazeera.[[308]](#cite_note-311)

[Boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), [karate](/wiki/Karate "Karate"), [kickboxing](/wiki/Kickboxing "Kickboxing"), [Muay Thai](/wiki/Muay_Thai "Muay Thai"), and [ju-jitsu](/wiki/Jujutsu "Jujutsu") are also popular. Less common sports are also gaining popularity. [Rugby](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football") is increasing in popularity, a rugby union is recognised by the Jordan Olympic Committee which supervises three national teams.[[309]](#cite_note-312) Although [cycling](/wiki/Cycling "Cycling") is not widespread, the sport is developing as a lifestyle and a new way to travel especially among the youth.[[310]](#cite_note-313) In 2014, a NGO [Make Life Skate Life](/wiki/Make_Life_Skate_Life "Make Life Skate Life") completed construction of the [7Hills Skatepark](/wiki/7Hills_Skatepark "7Hills Skatepark"), the first skatepark in the country located in [Downtown Amman](/wiki/Downtown_Amman "Downtown Amman").[[311]](#cite_note-314)
